title: "PDEs 3: Sobolev spaces"
author: "Liam Llamazares--Elias"
date: "2023-07-12"
categories: [Pdes]
description: "Density, trace and embeddings"
bibliography: biblio.bib
format:
  html:
    css: custom_theorems.css
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
    html-math-method: mathjax
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        <script>
        window.MathJax = {
          tex: {
            tags: "ams",
            macros: {
              A: "\\mathbb{A}",
              Aa: "\\mathcal{A}",
              Bb: "\\mathcal{B}",
              C: "\\mathbb{C}",
              Cc: "\\mathcal{C}",
              Dd: "\\mathcal{D}",
              E: "\\mathbb{E}",
              Ee: "\\mathcal{E}",
              F: "\\mathbb{F}",
              Ff: "\\mathcal{F}",
              Gg: "\\mathcal{G}",
              Hh: "\\mathcal{H}",
              II: "\\mathbb{I}",
              Id: "\\bm{I}",
              Ii: "\\mathcal{I}",
              Im: "\\bm{Im}",
              Jj: "\\mathcal{J}",
              K: "\\mathbb{K}",
              KL: ["\\rm{KL}\\left(#1 \\| #2\\right)", 2],
              Kk: "\\mathcal{K}",
              LL: "\\mathbb{L}",
              Ll: "\\mathcal{L}",
              M: "\\mathbb{M}",
              Mm: "\\mathcal{M}",
              N: "\\mathbb{N}",
              Nn: "\\mathcal{N}",
              Oo: "\\mathcal{O}",
              PP: "\\mathbb{P}",
              Pp: "\\mathcal{P}",
              Q: "\\mathbb{Q}",
              Qq: "\\mathcal{Q}",
              R: "\\mathbb{R}",
              Rr: "\\mathcal{R}",
              Ss: "\\mathcal{S}",
              T: "\\mathbb{T}",
              Th: "{\\bm{\\theta}}",
              Thh: "{\\bm{\\Theta}}",
              Tt: "\\mathcal{T}",
              Uu: "\\mathcal{U}",
              Vv: "\\mathcal{V}",
              W: "\\mathbb{W}",
              Ww: "\\mathcal{W}",
              X: "\\mathbb{X}",
              Xx: "\\mathcal{X}",
              Yy: "\\mathcal{Y}",
              Z: "\\mathbb{Z}",
              Zz: "\\mathcal{Z}",
              abs: ["{\\left| #1 \\right|}", 1],
              alf: "{\\alpha}",
              b: ["{\\bm{#1}}", 1],
              bm: ["\\boldsymbol{#1}", 1],
              br: ["\\left\\langle#1\\right\\rangle", 1],
              bt: "\\beta",
              co: ["\\overset{\\circ}{#1}", 1],
              d: "\\mathrm{d}",
              diag: ["\\operatorname{diag}\\left(#1\\right)", 1],
              dx: "\\,\\mathrm{d}x",
              dy: "\\,\\mathrm{d}y",
              eps: "\\varepsilon",
              et: "{\\bm{\\eta}}",
              ex: ["\\mathrm{exp}\\left(#1\\right)", 1],
              fk: ["\\mathfrak{#1}", 1],
              fracc: ["{#1}/{#2}", 2],
              frc: ["\\left(#2)^{-1}(#1\\right)", 2],
              iso: "\\xrightarrow{\\sim}",
              ker: "\\rm{ker}",
              mat: ["\\begin{bmatrix}{#1}\\end{bmatrix}", 1],
              mathbbm: ["\\mathbb{#1}", 1],
              mathds: ["\\mathbb{#1}", 1],
              norm: ["\\left\\lVert #1 \\right\\rVert", 1],
              om: "\\omega",
              postref: ["\\href{https://nowheredifferentiable.com/posts/#1/}{#3}", 3],
              proj: "\\mathrm{proj}",
              qb: ["\\left[#1\\right]", 1],
              qp: ["\\left(#1\\right)", 1],
              qt: ["\\left(#1\\right)", 1],
              red: ["{\\color{red}#1}", 1],
              restr: ["\\left.#1\\right|{#2}", 2],
              rm: ["\\mathrm{#1}", 1],
              set: ["\\left\\{#1\\right\\}", 1],
              star: "*",
              tl: ["\\widetilde{#1}", 1],
              tll: ["\\widetilde{#1}", 1],
              vol: "\\mathrm{vol}",
              wh: ["\\widehat{#1}", 1],
              x: "\\bm{x}",
              xxi: "{\\bm{\\xi}}",
              y: "\\bm{y}"
            }
          }
        };
        </script>
---

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
[Download PDF version](Sobolev%20spaces.pdf)
:::

##  Three line summary

- Given open $U \subset \R^d$ the Sobolev spaces $W^{k,p}(U)$ are **complete** spaces of ** weakly differentiable** functions.

- **Smooth functions are dense** in  $W^{k,p}(\Omega)$ if $\Omega$  is bounded and has $C^1$ boundary. This lets us manipulate Sobolev functions as if they were smooth and then take limits. In particular, we can **extend and restrict** functions to $\partial \Omega$.

- Functions in $W^{k,p}(\Omega)$ enjoy various inequalities. Cashing in differentiability for integrability we can **compactly embed**

    $$
    \begin{aligned}
    W^{k,p}(\Omega) \hookrightarrow L^q(\Omega).
    \end{aligned}
    $$

    Where $q\equiv q(d,k,p)$ decreases with the dimension $d$, increases with differentiability $k$ and integrability $p$, and is larger than $p$.

## Why should I care?

Sobolev spaces allow us to extend the notion of differentiability to a wider class of functions. The fact that these spaces are complete and compactly embedded in $L^q$ spaces is an important tool to extract convergent sub-sequences. This is useful when solving differential equations as a common technique is to take a Cauchy sequence whose limit is the solution to the equation.

## Notation

- In this post we will be dealing with functions over a variety of domains. To facilitate interpretation of the notation we will stick to the convention that $K$ is a compact set, $U, V$ are open sets, and $\Omega$ is an open bounded set with $C^1$ boundary.

- Given a topological space $X$ and a subset  $A \subset X$ we abbreviate $A$ is dense in  $X$ with the topology of  $X$ by

    $$
    \begin{aligned}
    \overline{A}=X.
    \end{aligned}
    $$

    We stress that in practice $X$ may be itself a subset of some larger space  $Y$ (for example $X= H^s(\R^d)$ and $Y= L^p(\R^d)$) . However, the above notation will always mean the closure with the topology of  $X$ and not  $Y$.

- A related notation is we will write given $a_n \in  A$

    $$
    \begin{aligned}
    \lim_{n \to \infty}a_n =x \in X.
    \end{aligned}
    $$

    To mean the limit in the topology of $X$.

- Given two sets $A,B$ we write  $A \Subset B$ and say that **$A$ is compactly included in  $B$** if $\overline{A}$ is compact and **strictly** included in $B$.

- We also write

    $$
    \begin{aligned}
    A+B=\set{x+y:x \in A, y \in  B} .
    \end{aligned}
    $$

- Given a topological vector space $X$ we write $X'$ for the dual of  $X$ and denote given  $w \in X', \varphi \in X$ the duality pairing as

    $$
    \begin{aligned}
    (\varphi,w):= w(\varphi) .
    \end{aligned}
    $$

- We will always write $\alpha$ for a multi-index $\alpha \in \N^d$ and use the notation

    $$
    \begin{aligned}
    D^\alpha f := \partial _1^{\alpha_1}\cdots \partial _d^{\alpha_d}.
    \end{aligned}
    $$

    In the case $\alpha =0$ we use the convention $D^0 =f$.

- Given  a space of functions $X$ with domain $A$ and $B \subset A$ we write

    $$
    \begin{aligned}
    \restr{X}{B}:=\set{\restr{f}{B}: f\in X} .
    \end{aligned}
    $$

- Given two quantities $M,N$ we write $M \lesssim N$ to mean that there exists some constant $C$ independent of  $M$ and  $N$ such that  $M \leq C N$.

- We write $B_r(x)$ for the ball centered at  $x$ with radius  $r$ and  $B_r$ if $x=0$. The space where the ball is contained depending on context.

## Introduction

Hi everyone, welcome back to another post on our series on PDEs. This post goes into some depth on the main space of functions we will work with. Since the post is rather long (yes it has appendices, no that wasn't planned) I decided to include a pdf at the end of the post in case it is easier to navigate. That said, in the future we will want to solve a differential equation of the form

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Ll u = f\quad  \mathrm{ on  }\quad  D .
\end{aligned}
$$ {#eq-pde1}

Where $D$ is some domain in $\R^d$. In a previous post on the [Fourier transform](<../../PDEs/1. The Fourier transform, tempered distributions and Sobolev spaces/index.qmd>) we saw how to define the Sobolev spaces $H^s(D)$ when $D$ is the whole Euclidean space $\R^d$ or the torus $\T^d$. These spaces correspond to $s$-times weakly differentiable functions and we saw how these spaces could help us solve ([-@eq-pde1]).  However, in practice $D$ may be an open set in $\R^d$ or even some $d$-dimensional manifold with a boundary condition

$$
\begin{aligned}
\restr{u}{\partial  D}= g.
\end{aligned}
$$ {#eq-bc}

Note that equation ([-@eq-bc]) is a priori ill-defined as the Lebesgue measure of $\partial D \subset \R^d$ is zero.     Thus, it is necessary to extend the theory to a wider class of domains and to explain what we mean by the restriction of a function to its boundary of definition.

### A first attempt

Suppose for example $D=U$ is an open set and $u: U \to \R$. Then, we can try to define $H^s(U)$ using our knowledge of $H^s(\R^d)$ by:

1. Extending $u$ by zero outside of $U$ to form

    $$
    \begin{aligned}
    \tilde{u}(x):=\begin{cases}
    u(x) & \quad x \in U     \\
    0    & \quad x \not\in U
    \end{cases}.
    \end{aligned}
    $$

2. Studying if $\tilde{u} \in  H^s(\R^d)$. That is, as we saw in the [previous post](<../../PDEs/1. The Fourier transform, tempered distributions and Sobolev spaces/index.qmd>), checking if

    $$
    \begin{aligned}
    \norm{\tilde{u}}_{H^s(\R^d)}^2= \int_{\R^d}\br{\xi }^{2s}\wh{\tilde{u}}(\xi )^2 \d \xi < \infty
    \end{aligned}
    $$

3. Saying that $u \in H^s(U)$ if and only if $\tilde{u} \in H^s(\R^d)$.

However, this runs into problems as is shown in the following example:

::: {#exm-1}

Let $U=(0,1)$ and take $u: (0,1) \to \R$ defined to be identically equal to  $1$. Note that  $u \in C^\infty(U)$ so we expect that $u \in H^s((0,1))$ for all $s \in \R$. However, it holds that $\tilde{u}= {1}_{(0,1)}$ with

$$
\begin{aligned}
\wh{\tilde{u}}(\xi )=\frac{1-e^{-2 \pi i \xi }}{2 \pi i \xi }.
\end{aligned}
$$

:::

As we can see, by substituting in our naive definition of $H^s(U)$ gives that $u \in H^s((0,1))$ if and only if $s< \frac{1}{2}$. Thus, our program of extending $u$ by zero and studying the regularity of the extension is not going to work. The reason for this is that, by extending by zero we introduce a discontinuity on $\tilde{u}$ at the boundary of $U$.

### A second approach

Alternatively, we could also define

$$
\begin{aligned}
H^s(U)= \restr{H^s(\R^d)}{U}:= \set{\restr{f}{u}: f \in H^s(\R^d)} .
\end{aligned}
$$

As we will see later (@exr-restriction) this is a better approach. However, it is not optimal as it requires some conditions on $U$. For example, if $U$ is not smooth we cannot assert that  $\restr{C^\infty(\R^d)}{U}= C^\infty(U)$.

## Test functions and distributions

%

### Seminorms and their topologies

We need a new approach. Motivated as in the previous post by [duality](<../../PDEs/1. The Fourier transform, tempered distributions and Sobolev spaces/index.qmd>) we should begin by defining what is meant by a weak derivative of a function $u$ in $L^p(U)$. If $u,\varphi$ are smooth functions then we have by integration by parts

$$
\begin{aligned}
(\varphi, D^\alpha u):= \int_{D} \varphi D^\alpha u  = (-1)^{\abs{\alpha} }\int_{D}(D^{\alpha} \varphi) u + \text{ boundary effects}  .
\end{aligned}
$$

In the previous post, we used that

- If $D =\R^d$ we can take as our test functions $\varphi \in  \Ss(\R^d)$ and use that $\varphi$ multiplied by any function in $L^2$ ($u$ and its derivatives) vanish at infinity to get rid of the boundary effects.

- If $D =\T^d$ we can take as our test functions $\varphi \in  C^\infty(\T^d)$ as the boundary effect of periodic functions cancels out.

To obtain this cancellation on a general open $D$ we need to impose that our test function  $\varphi$ vanishes in a neighborhood of the boundary. That is we need our test functions to have compact support.

::: {#def-1}

Let $U$ be an open set, then we define  $C_c^\infty(U)$ to be the space of smooth functions whose support is some compact set $K \subset U$.     

:::

Another notation for $C^\infty_c(U)$ is $\Dd(U)$ and it is often called the space of **test functions** for reasons we will later see.  Given a compact subset $K \subset U$  we define for each $k \in \N$

::: {#def-2}

Given a compact subset $K$ of an open set  $U$ we define

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_c^\infty(K):= \set{\varphi \in C_c^\infty(U): \supp{\varphi} \subset K } .
\end{aligned}
$$

We endow  $C_c^\infty(K)$ with the [topology generated  by the countable family of seminorms](<../../PDEs/1. The Fourier transform, tempered distributions and Sobolev spaces/index.qmd>)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\norm{\varphi}_{C^k(K)}:= \sup_{x \in K} \sum_{\abs{\alpha}\leq k } \abs{D^\alpha \varphi}, \quad  k \in \N_{0}
\end{aligned}
$$ {#eq-local-smooth-norms}

:::

Later we will need to generate topologies when the family of seminorms is uncountable.

::: {#def-3}

Let $X$ be a vector space and let $\mathcal{P}$ be a family of seminorms on $X$. Then we define $\tau_\Pp$, the **topology generated by $\mathcal{P}$**, to be the topology generated by the local basis

$$
\begin{aligned}
B^\rho(x,\eps):=x+\rho^{-1}(B_\eps), \quad \rho \in \Pp, \eps >0
\end{aligned}
$$

:::

That is, $\set{B^\rho(x,\eps): rho \in \Pp, \eps>0}$ is a sub-basis of $\tau_\Pp$. Equivalently, $U$ is open if and only if it is an arbitrary union of finite intersections.

The above topology is equivalent to the initial topology generated by the family

$$
\begin{aligned}
\set{\rho(\cdot-x): x\in X, \rho \in \Pp}.
\end{aligned}
$$

We include some exercises to help the reader get more used to the topology that arises. I recommended trying to solve them for a few minutes before checking the hints.

::: {#exr-tvs-ex}

Write $\tau_{\Pp}$ for the topology generated by $\Pp$, Show that $\tau_{\Pp}$ is the coarsest topology that makes $X$ into a topological vector space ([TVS](<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Topological_vector_space>)).

:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}

The fact that $(X,\tau_\mathcal{P})$ is a TVS follows from the triangle inequality and homogeneity of seminorms. The fact that it is the coarsest that makes $\rho$ continuous is that $\rho^{-1}(B_\eps)$ must be an open neighbourhood of the origin and in a TVS, by continuity of the sum, translation of an open set must be open.

:::

::: {#exr-convex-ex}

Show that $(X,\tau_\mathcal{P})$ is locally convex. That is, every point has a local basis of convex sets

:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}

Show that $\rho^{-1}(B_\eps)$ is convex.

:::

::: {#exr-convergence-tvs}

Show that the topology $(X,\tau_\mathcal{P})$ is determined by the following property.

- Given a [net](<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Net_(mathematics)#:~:text=%5Bedit%5D-,Any,-function%20whose%20domain>) $x_\bullet\in (X,\tau_\Pp)$  it holds that

    $$
    \begin{aligned}
    \lim x_\bullet =x \in  X  \iff      \rho(x_\bullet-x)\to 0 \quad\forall \rho \in \Pp.
    \end{aligned}
    $$

:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}

The topology of any topological space is completely determined by the convergence of nets. So it is enough to show that the property holds. The implication holds by the continuity of  $\rho$, the reverse follows from being able to fit $x_\bullet$ into any basic set $x+\rho(B_\eps)$.

:::

::: {#exr-1}

Show that $C_c^\infty(K)$ is complete and thus a [Fréchet](<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fr%C3%A9chet_space#:~:text=locally%20convex%20metrizable%20topological%20vector%20space>) space.

:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}

The topology is [metrizable](<../../PDEs/1. The Fourier transform, tempered distributions and Sobolev spaces/index.qmd>) as the family of seminorms is countable
Use @exr-convergence-tvs  to show that if $\varphi_n \in C_c^\infty(K)$ is Cauchy then the sequence of derivatives $D^\alpha \varphi_n$ converge uniformly to $\varphi^{(\alpha)} \in C_c^0(K)$ for all $\alpha$. It remains to show that

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varphi^{(0)}= \lim_{n \to \infty} \varphi_n \in  C_c^\infty(K).
\end{aligned}
$$

To do so use the fundamental theorem of calculus and induction.

:::

Using $C_c^\infty(K)$ as a stepping stone we can build a topology on $C_c^\infty(U)$. We use the approach in [Terence Tao's blog post on distributions](<https://terrytao.wordpress.com/2009/04/19/245c-notes-3-distributions/#:~:text=is%20clearly%20a-,vector,-space.%20Now%20we>). Let us call a seminorm on $C_c^\infty(U)$ **restrictable** if it is a continuous function on $C_c^\infty(K)$ for all $K \subset U$.     

::: {#def-4}

The topology on $C_c^\infty(U)$ is the one generated by all the restrictable seminorms on $C_c^\infty(U)$. We call this topology the **smooth topology**.

:::

::: {#exr-2}

Give an infinite restrictable family of seminorms.

:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}

Valid answers include all the $L^p(U)$ and $C^k(U)$ norms.

:::

::: {#exr-3}

Show that  $C_c^\infty(\Omega)$ with the smooth topology is a locally convex topological vector space (LCTVS).

:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}

See Exercises @exr-tvs-ex-@exr-convex-ex

:::

::: {#exr-local-convergence name="Smooth convergence is equal to local convergence"}

Show that $\varphi_n \to \varphi \in C_c^\infty(U)$ if and only if
there exists a compact set $K$ such that for all $n$ the support of $\varphi_n$ and $\varphi$ are in  $K$ and

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{n \to \infty}\varphi_n  =\varphi \in  C_c^\infty(K).
\end{aligned}
$$

:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}

Given any sequence $\mathbf{a}=\set{a_j}_{j\in \mathbb{N}} \in \R_+$ and an increasing set of compact sets $K_j$ with $U=\bigcup_{j\in \mathbb{N}} K_j$ show that

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_{\mathbf{a}}(\varphi):=\sup _{j \in \N}{a_j} \sum_{|\alpha| \leq j}\norm{D^\alpha \varphi(x)}_{L^\infty(U\setminus K_j)}.
\end{aligned}
$$

Is a restrictable seminorm. Why does this prevent the support of $\varphi_n$ escaping to infinity? Now knowing all functions are supported in some $K$ use that $\norm{\cdot }_{C^k(K)}$ is restrictable to conclude the proof.

:::

::: {#exr-4 name="Completeness"}

Show that $C_c^\infty(\Omega)$ with the smooth topology is complete.

:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}

Given a Cauchy net $\varphi_{\bullet} \in C_c^\infty(U)$ show as in @exr-local-convergence that the support of  $\varphi_{\bullet}$ cannot escape a compact set $K$. Conclude using the completeness on  $C_c^\infty(K)$ and @exr-convergence-tvs. 

:::

::: {#exr-5}

Show that $C_c^\infty(\Omega)$ with the smooth topology is Hausdorff.

:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}

The initial topology of a set of functions that separates points is Hausdorff.

:::

::: {#rem-1}

The topology on $C_c^\infty(U)$ is **not** metrizable (note the family of seminorms used to generate it is not countable) and as a result $C_c^\infty(U)$ **is not** a Fréchet space. This and more can be found in [@leoni2017first] page 286.

:::

The construction of the topology on $C_c^\infty(U)$ is a bit technical a more intuitive construction would be to define the family of seminorms

$$
\begin{aligned}
\norm{\varphi}_j := \sup_{\abs{\alpha} \leq j}  \norm{D^\alpha \varphi}_{L^\infty(U)} ,\quad \forall j \in \N.
\end{aligned}
$$

And then define the topology on $C_c^\infty(U)$ to be the one generated by this family of seminorms. This has the following problem.

::: {#exr-6}

Show that $C_c^\infty(U)$ with the topology generated by $\norm{\cdot }_j$ is not complete.

:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}

Construct a  sequence that is Cauchy with respect to every $\norm{\cdot }_j$ whose support escapes to infinity.

:::

Using the smooth topology we can now work with the dual of $C_c^\infty(U)$

::: {#def-5}

We define the space of **distributions** to be

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Dd'(U):= (C_c^\infty(U))'.
\end{aligned}
$$

And give it the [weak-$\star$](<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Weak_topology>) topology.

:::

::: {#rem-2}

The inclusions

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_c^\infty(U) \hookrightarrow C_c^\infty(\R^d) \hookrightarrow \Ss(\R^d)
\end{aligned}
$$

are continuous. As a result, $\Ss'(\R^d) \hookrightarrow \Dd'(U)$. That is, distributions are a larger or more general class than tempered distributions.

:::

Of course, not all smooth functions have compact support. Other (in this case Fréchet) spaces of smooth functions are

$$
\begin{aligned}
C^\infty(U)                & :=\set{\varphi: \norm{\varphi}_j:= \sup_{\abs{\alpha} \leq j}  \norm{D^\alpha \varphi}_{L^\infty(U)}<\infty,\quad \forall k \in \mathbb{N} }                  \\
	C^\infty_{\mathrm{loc}}(U) & :=\set{\varphi: \norm{\varphi}_{k,K}:= \sup_{\abs{\alpha} \leq k}  \norm{D^\alpha \varphi}_{L^\infty(K)}<\infty,\quad \forall k \in \mathbb{N}, K \subset U }
\end{aligned}
$$

Where, we give them respectively the topologies generated by $\norm{\cdot}_k$ and $\norm{\cdot}_{k,K}$.

An equivalent characterization of $\Dd'(U)$ [@taylor2013partial, page 241] is that,  $\omega \in  \Dd'(U)$ if and only if for all $\varphi \in C_c^\infty(U)$ the operator $\varphi w$ defined by 
$$
\begin{aligned}
(f, \varphi w):= (\varphi f, w), \quad\forall f \in C^\infty(U),
\end{aligned}
$$ {#eq-equiv}

is continuous on $C^\infty(U)$.

### Locally integrable functions as distributions

%
In our post on the Fourier transform [we saw](<../../PDEs/1. The Fourier transform, tempered distributions and Sobolev spaces/index.qmd>) that integrable functions could naturally be considered as tempered distributions. The analogous is true for distributions. In this case, since our test functions are compactly supported, the functions we pair them up with only need to be locally integrable. We recall the definition

::: {#def-6}

Given $p\in [1,\infty]$ we write $L^p_{\mathrm{loc}}(U)$ for the space of **locally $p$ integrable functions**,

$$
\begin{aligned}
L^p_{\mathrm{loc}}(U):= \set{f: \norm{f}_{L^p(K)}< \infty, \,\text{   for all compact }  K \subset U}.
\end{aligned}
$$

And endow it with the [topology generated by the family of seminorms](<../../PDEs/1. The Fourier transform, tempered distributions and Sobolev spaces/index.qmd#eq-seminormsgivemetric>)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_{K_n,p}(f):= \norm{f}_{L^p(K_n)}.
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $K_n$ are selected such that
$\bigcup_{n \in \N} K_n =U$.

:::

Note that $L^q_{\mathrm{loc}}(U) \subset L^p_{\mathrm{loc} }(U)$ for all $q \leq p$.

::: {#exr-7}

Show that $L^p_{\mathrm{loc} }(U)$ is a Fréchet space.

:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}

The topology is [metrizable](<../../PDEs/1. The Fourier transform, tempered distributions and Sobolev spaces/index.qmd>) as the family of seminorms is countable. By @exr-convergence-tvs  if $f_n \in L^p_{\mathrm{loc}}(U)$ is Cauchy then $f_n$ is Cauchy for all  $L^p(K)$ $D^\alpha \varphi_n$ so converges to some $f_K \in L^p(K)$. Show that $f_K= \restr{f}{K}$ where $f \in L^p_{\mathrm{loc}}(U)$    to conclude the proof.

:::

::: {#thm-motivation name="Locally integrable functions as distributions"}

The mapping

$$
\begin{aligned}
T:L^1_{\mathrm{loc}}(U) & \hookrightarrow  \Dd'(U) \\
u                       & \longmapsto     T_u
.
\end{aligned}
$$

Defined by

$$
\begin{aligned}
(\varphi,T_u):= \int_{U}\varphi u   , \quad\forall \varphi\in C_c^\infty(U).
\end{aligned}
$$ {#eq-duality}

Is injective and continuous.    

:::

::: {.proof}

We begin by showing that $T_u \in \Dd '(U)$. Firstly, the integral in ([-@eq-duality]) is finite as $\varphi$ has compact support. Secondly, if we write $K$ for the support of  $\varphi$,   it holds that

$$
\begin{aligned}
(f,\varphi u)= \int_{K}\varphi f u \leq \norm{u}_{L^1(K)} \norm{\varphi f}_{C^0_c(K)} .
\end{aligned}
$$

Which, by our equivalent characterization of $\Dd'(U)$  in ([-@eq-equiv]) shows that $T_u \in  \Dd'(U)$. We now show that the identification is injective.  That is, if

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{U}\varphi u=0 , \quad\forall \varphi \in C_c^\infty(U) .
\end{aligned}
$$

Then $u=0$. Consider a compact set $K \subset U$ and write  $g$ for the extension of $\mathrm{sign}(u)$ by zero outside of  $K$. Clearly $g \subset L^1(\R^d)$. Consider an approximation to the identity $\phi_n$ (see Appendix @sec-smooth-section ) and set

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varphi_n :=  g*\phi_n.
\end{aligned}
$$

By the approximation and smoothing of Propositions @prp-app-pn-@prp-smooth we obtain a bounded sequence with $\varphi_n \subset C_c^\infty(U)$ for $n$ large enough and such that

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{n \to \infty}\varphi_n =g \in L^1(\R^d) ; \quad \norm{\varphi_n}_{L^\infty(\R^d)} \leq \norm{g}_{L^\infty(\R^d)}=1.
\end{aligned}
$$

By the first part of the above, we may take a subsequence $\varphi_{n_k}$ converging to $g$ almost everywhere, and by the second we may apply the dominated convergence theorem to obtain that

$$
\begin{aligned}
0= \lim_{k \to \infty}\int_{U}\varphi_{n_k} u=\int_{K}\abs{u}.
\end{aligned}
$$

As a result, $u=0$ vanishes on  $K$. Since $K$ was any compact subset of $U$  and every open set can be written as a union of compact sets we conclude that $u=0$ as desired.
The continuity of  $T$ follows from the estimate

$$
\begin{aligned}
(\varphi,T_{u} -T_v)\leq \norm{\varphi}_{C_c^k(K)}\norm{u-v}_{L^1_{\mathrm{loc}}(U)}.
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $\varphi \in C_c^\infty(U)$ has support $K$ (remember we are considering the weak-$\star$ topology on $\Dd'(U)$).

:::

Due to the above immersion, we will naturally consider $L^1_{\mathrm{loc}}(U)$ as a subspace of $\Dd'(U)$.    In particular, any subspaces of $L^1_{\mathrm{loc} }(U)$ such as $L^p(U)$ or  $C^\infty(U)$ can also be considered as distributions. 

::: {#exr-8}

Show that the $L^1_{\mathrm{loc}}(U)$ is not closed in $\Dd'(U)$  .

:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}

Show that an approximation to the identity converges to a Dirac delta $\delta _0 \in \Dd'(U)$. However $\delta _0 \not\in L^1_{\mathrm{loc}}(U)$.

:::

### Support of a distribution

In the continuous case, the support of a function is well-defined as the smallest closed set outside of which the function is zero. However, when working with an equivalence class of functions the definition must be amended (consider for example the support of $0=1_{\Q}$). This is resolved by the following definitions.

::: {#def-7}

We say that a distribution $w \in \Dd'(U)$ **vanishes** on $V \subset U$ if

$$
\begin{aligned}
(\varphi, w)=0 , \quad\forall \varphi \in C_c^\infty(U) \text{ with } \supp{\varphi} \subset V  .
\end{aligned}
$$

And write

$$
\begin{aligned}
w =0 \text{ on } V.
\end{aligned}
$$

:::

If a function vanishes on a collection of sets it also vanishes on their union, this extends to distributions.

::: {#lem-biggest-vanish}

Let  $\set{U_\alpha}_{\alpha \subset I}$ be a collection of open sets in $U$ and suppose that

$$
\begin{aligned}
w=0 \text{ on  } U_\alpha , \quad\forall \alpha \in I.
\end{aligned}
$$

Then $w$ vanished on  $U:=\bigcup_{\alpha \in  I}U_\alpha$.

:::

::: {.proof}

Let $\varphi$ have support in $U$. Then,     by compactness, we can extract a finite covering $\set{U_i}_{i=1}^n$ of $\supp{\varphi}$. Let $\set{\rho _i}_{i=1}^n$ be a partition of unity  subordinate to $U_i$ (see Appendix @sec-local-to-global). Then

$$
\begin{aligned}
(\varphi,\omega)=\qt{\sum_{i=1}^n \rho _i \varphi,w }=\sum_{i=1}^n (\rho _i \varphi,w )=0 .
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\varphi$ was any test function supported in $U$ this concludes the proof.

:::

By the just proved @lem-biggest-vanish we see that there is a largest set on which $w$ vanishes. As a result, we can make the following definition.

::: {#def-support-def}

Let $w \in C_c^\infty(U)$ and let  $V$ be the largest open set on which  $w$ vanishes. Then, we define the **support** of $w$ as

$$
\begin{aligned}
\supp{w}= V^c .
\end{aligned}
$$

:::

Since $L^1_{\mathrm{loc}}(U)$ is naturally included in  $\Dd'(U)$ we obtain in particular the definition of support of a function $f \in L^1_{\mathrm{loc}}(U)$.

::: {#exr-9}

If  $f \in L^1_{\mathrm{loc}}(U)$ the support of $f$ is the complementary of the largest open set on which  $f$ is  $0$ almost everywhere. In particular, if $f$ is continuous, the (distributional) support of  $f$ coincides with the classical support of  $f$. 

:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}

We saw in @thm-motivation that $f$ is  $0$ almost everywhere on some open set if and only if it integrates to $0$ against any test function on the open set. This shows the first part and the second follows immediately.

:::

## Sobolev spaces

Now that we have built the space of distributions we can define weak derivatives of test functions just as we did with tempered distributions.

::: {#def-8}

Given $w \in \Dd'(U)$ we define the **(weak) $\alpha$-th derivative** by

$$
\begin{aligned}
(\varphi,D^\alpha w):=(-1)^{\abs{\alpha} }(D^\alpha \varphi, w), \quad \forall \varphi \in C_c^\infty(U).
\end{aligned}
$$

:::

::: {#exr-10}

Show that $D^\alpha w \in\Dd'(U)$.

:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}

Show that $D^\alpha$ is continuous on $\Dd(U)$ by using that $\norm{\cdot }_{C^k(U)}$ are restrictable seminorms. Conclude by using that $D^\alpha$ defined on $\Dd'(U)$ is the [adjoint](<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Transpose_of_a_linear_map>) of $D^\alpha$ defined on $\Dd(U)$

:::

A prerequisite for the definition to make sense is that the notion corresponds to that of classical derivative.

::: {#exr-11}

Let $u \in C_{\mathrm{loc}}^1(U)$ have classical derivatives $u^{(i)}$. Then $u^{(i)}= \partial_i u$.

:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}

By definition of weak derivatives and the chain rule, we have the distributional equality

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{u^{(i)}}-T_{\partial _i u}=T_{u^{(i)}-\partial _i u}=0 \in \Dd'(U)   .
\end{aligned}
$$

The result follows by the injectivity of $T$.

:::

::: {#def-sobolev-def name="Sobolev spaces"}

Given an open set $U \subset \R^d$, $k \in \N$ and $p \in [1,\infty]$  we define the **Sobolev space**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W^{k,p}(U):=\set{ u: D^{\alpha}u \in L^p(U) \hookrightarrow \Dd'(U), \quad\forall \abs{\alpha}\leq k   } .
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $L^p(U)$ is identified as a subspace of  $\Dd'(U)$ by @thm-motivation.  We give $W^{k,p}(U)$ the norm

$$
\begin{aligned}
\norm{u}_{W^{k,p}(U)}&:=\sum_{\abs{\alpha}\leq k } \norm{D^\alpha u}_{L^p(U)}.
\end{aligned}
$$

:::

That is, $W^{k,p}(U)$ is the space of $k$-times (weakly) differentiable functions with derivatives in $L^p(U)$. An equivalent norm that is also sometimes used is

$$
\begin{aligned}
\norm{u}_{W^{k,p}(U)}\sim \sum_{j=0}^k \norm{\nabla^j u}_{L^p(U\to \R^{d^j})} .
\end{aligned}
$$

The local Sobolev spaces $W^{k,p}_{\mathrm{loc}}(U)$ are defined similarly, where we now only require that

$$
\begin{aligned}
D^\alpha u \in L^p_{\text{loc}}(U) , \quad\forall \abs{\alpha}\leq k .
\end{aligned}
$$

And now generate the topology by local seminorms analogously to $L^p_{\mathrm{loc}}(U)$.

::: {#thm-completeness name="Completeness of Sobolev spaces"}

For all  $k \in \N$ and $p \in [1,\infty]$ both  $W^{k,p}(U)$ and $W^{k,p}_{\mathrm{loc}}(U)$ are Banach spaces. If $p \in (1,\infty)$ these spaces are also reflexive.

:::

::: {.proof}

Let $\set{u_n}_{n=1}^\infty$ be a Cauchy sequence in  $W^{k,p}(U)$. Then, by definition of the norm on $W^{k,p}(U)$ the sequence of derivatives $D^\alpha u_n$ is Cauchy in $L^p(U)$ for  each $\abs{\alpha}\leq k$ and since $L^p(U)$ is complete, converge to some functions $u^{\alpha}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{n \to \infty} D^\alpha u_n = u^{(\alpha)} \in L^p(U) , \quad\forall \abs{\alpha} \leq k  .
\end{aligned}
$$

To conclude, it suffices to show that  $u:= u^{(0)}$ verifies

$$
\begin{aligned}
D^\alpha u= u^{(\alpha)}.
\end{aligned}
$$

This holds as, for every test function $\varphi \in  \Dd(U)$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{U} u^{(\alpha)}\varphi =\lim_{n \to \infty}\int_{U} u_n^{(\alpha)}\varphi=\lim_{n \to \infty}(-1)^{\abs{\alpha} }\int_{U} u_nD^\alpha\varphi=(-1)^{\abs{\alpha} }\int_{U} uD^\alpha\varphi  .
\end{aligned}
$$

Where the first and last inequality follows from the continuous immersion of $L^p(U)$ in $\Dd'(U)$ (Hölder's inequality). Reflexivity follows from the fact that the mapping

$$
\begin{aligned}
T: W^{k,p}(U) & \longrightarrow (L^p(U))_{\abs{\alpha}\leq k }
	\\
	u             & \longmapsto     (D^{\alpha}(u))_{\abs{\alpha}\leq k }
	.
\end{aligned}
$$

Is an isometry, so $\Im(T)$ is closed in the reflexive Banach space $(L^p(U))_{\abs{\alpha}\leq k }
$ and thus reflexive (see [@brezis2011functional] page 70).
The case of $W^{k,p}_{\mathrm{loc}}(U)$ is proved identically now working with the local seminorms.

:::

We now show some relevant properties of the weak derivative all of which are to be expected knowing the classical case. These can be greatly generalized with tools we will later develop.

::: {#prp-properties name="Properties"}

Let $u \in W^{k,p}(U)$. Then it holds that

1. Leibniz rule: given $\varphi \in C_c^1(U)$ it holds that

    $$
    \begin{aligned}
    \partial _{i} (u \varphi)= \partial_i u \varphi+ u \partial _i \varphi .
    \end{aligned}
    $$

2. The translation $\tau_y u(x):=u(x-y) \in W^{k,p}(U)$ with $D^\alpha \tau_y u = \tau_y D^\alpha u$.

3. Let $u \in W^{k,p}(\R^d)$ and $v \in L^1(\R^d)$. Then

    $$
    \begin{aligned}
    D^\alpha (v*u)= v* D^\alpha u
    \end{aligned}
    $$

:::

::: {.proof}

Points 1 and 2 follow by the definition of weak derivative and the relevant properties for classical derivatives. The third property follows from the second as, by Fubini,

$$
\begin{aligned}
& (v*u,D^\alpha\varphi )  = \int_{\R^d}v(y)\qt{\int_{\R^d}D^\alpha \varphi(x) u(x-y) \d x } \d y \\&
	=(-1)^{\abs{\alpha} } \int_{\R^d}v(y)  \qt{\int_{\R^d}\varphi(x) D^\alpha u(x-y) \d x } \d y             =(-1)^{\abs{\alpha} }(v*D^\alpha u, \varphi).
\end{aligned}
$$

:::

A natural question is what relationship there is between Sobolev functions and classical derivatives. For example, in $1$ dimension a classical result is that  $u \in W^{1,1}(a,b)$ if and only if $u$ is absolutely continuous and has derivative almost everywhere. A more general result is as follows.

::: {#prp-1 name="Absolute continuity on lines"}

The following are equivalent

- $u \in W^{1,p}_{\mathrm{loc}}(U)$.

- There exists a representative of $u$ in $L^p_{\mathrm{loc}}(U)$ that is almost everywhere differentiable with classical derivatives $u^{(i)} \in L^p_{\mathrm{loc}}(U)$ and, given $V \Subset U$ it holds that this representative is absolutely continuous on almost all (with respect to the Lebesgue measure on $\R^{d-1}$) line segments in $V$ parallel to the coordinate axis.

The above holds true if we replace $W^{1,p}_{\mathrm{loc}}(U),L^p_{\mathrm{loc}}(U)$ with their none local counterparts $W^{1,p}(U),L^p(U)$.

:::

We omit the proof which can be found in [@Juha2023] pages $39-43$. The next exercise show that, perhaps somewhat unexpectedly, to have $u \in  W^{1,p}(U)$ it is not sufficient to require that $u$ is differentiable almost everywhere with integrable derivatives.

::: {#exm-2}

The [devil's staircase](<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cantor_function>) $c$ is differentiable almost everywhere with $c'=0$. 

:::

As a result, if $c \in W^{1,p}(0,1)$ then $u$ would be constant (which it is not). In fact, $c \not\in W^{1,p}(0,1)$ as it is not absolutely continuous.

## Smooth approximation of Sobolev functions

The definition of weak derivative requires one to integrate against smooth functions whenever trying to prove some property holds. This is somewhat cumbersome. One would much rather

1. Work pretending all Sobolev functions are (classically) smooth.

2. Manipulate them according to the standard rules of calculus.

3. Obtain a result that holds for all Sobolev functions (and not just the classically smooth ones).

This process can be rigorously justified by the density of various spaces of smooth functions in Sobolev spaces. Or to use Tao's terminology, by giving ourselves [an epsilon of room](<https://terrytao.wordpress.com/2009/02/28/tricks-wiki-give-yourself-an-epsilon-of-room/>).

In this section, we prove the relevant results. These rely heavily on the analogous density results for functions in $L^p(U)$ (see Appendix @sec-smooth-section). As a result, they will not when $p=\infty$. We start without making any assumptions on $U$ and obtain two local-type results. Throughout this section we will often be switching between different open sets and, if following the proofs, making some drawings is recommended.

::: {#thm-local name="Local approximation by smooth functions"}

Let  $u \in W^{k,p}(U)$ for $p < \infty$, denote its extension by zero $\tilde{u}$  and let $\set{\phi_n}_{n=1}^\infty$ be an approximation to unity. Then

$$
\begin{aligned}
u=\lim_{n \to \infty} \tilde{u}*\phi_n  \in W^{k,p}(V) , \quad\forall V \Subset U.
\end{aligned}
$$

As a result, for any $V \Subset U$,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{\restr{C_c^\infty(\R^d)}{V}}=\restr{W^{k,p}(U)}{V}\quad \text{ and } \quad \overline{\restr{C_c^\infty(\R^d)}{U}}=W_{\mathrm{loc}}^{k,p}(U)
\end{aligned}
$$

:::

::: {.proof}

Given a compactly embedded set $V \Subset U$ we can take $n$ large enough so that  $V+  B \qt{0, \frac{1}{n}} \subset U$ and as a result, the convolution  $\varphi_n:= \tilde{u} * \phi_n$  verifies (see Observation @rem-local-smoothing)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varphi_n= u* \phi_n \quad  \text{ on  } V .
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus, by the third property @prp-properties, for all $\abs{\alpha} \leq k$

$$
\begin{aligned}
D^\alpha \varphi_n = D^\alpha u * \phi_n \quad \text{ on } V.
\end{aligned}
$$

Taking limits we conclude from @prp-app-pn that

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{n \to \infty}D^\alpha \varphi_n =D^\alpha u \in L^p(V) .
\end{aligned}
$$

To conclude density of $C_c^\infty(\R^d)$ in $\restr{W^{k,p}(U)}{V}$  consider $K$ such that

$$
\begin{aligned}
V \subset K; \quad  K+ B_{1 /n}\subset U.
\end{aligned}
$$

And a bump function $\eta_V \in C_c^\infty(\R^d)$ that is equal to $1$ on  $V$ and is supported in $K$ (this is possible by Uryshon's lemma and a convolution). Then,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varphi_{V,n}:= \varphi_n \eta _V \in C_c^\infty(\R^d); \quad \lim_{n \to \infty}\varphi_{V,n}=u \in W^{k,p}(V).
\end{aligned}
$$

The density of $C_c^\infty(\R^d)$ in  $W^{k,p}_{\mathrm{loc}}(U)$ follows by taking $V_n$ converging to  $U$ and  $u_n:= \varphi_{V_n,n}$ as then, for every compactly included $W \Subset  U$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{n \to \infty}u_{n}=u \in W^{k,p}(W) .
\end{aligned}
$$

This concludes the proof as by @exr-convergence-tvs local convergence is equivalent to convergence on every compactly included subset.

:::

::: {#rem-3}

Note that, without further assumptions on $U$ it is impossible to get a global approximation by smooth functions defined on all of $\R^d$. This is because, the convolution $f* \phi_n$ can only be defined on

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{1/n}:= \set{x \in U: d(x,\partial U)> \frac{1}{n}} .
\end{aligned}
$$

:::

The above issue disappears when $U =\R^d$. This shows that

::: {#thm-approx-rd name="Global approximation in $\R^d$"}

It holds that for all $p<\infty$,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{C_c^\infty(\R^d)}=W^{k,p}(\R^d).
\end{aligned}
$$

:::

::: {.proof}

Let $\eta   \in C_c^\infty(\R^d)$ be equal to  $1$ on  $B_1$ and set $\eta  _n(x):=\eta  (x /n)$.  Then, given $u \in W^{k,p}(\R^n)$ and a smooth approximation to unity $\phi_n$ we obtain that, by the triangle inequality

$$
\begin{aligned}
u= \lim_{n \to \infty}(u*\phi_n)\eta  _n \in W^{k,p}(\R^d).
\end{aligned}
$$

:::

The following Theorem shows a global-type approximation.

::: {#thm-meyers name="Meyers-Serrin: local till boundary approximation"}

Let $U \subset \R^d$ be open, then for all $p<\infty$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{C^\infty_{\mathrm{loc}}(U)\cap W^{k,p}(U)}=W^{k,p}(U).
\end{aligned}
$$

:::

::: {.proof}

The proof is an instructive way of using a partition of unity to piece together a local result (in this case @thm-local) to get a global one. Let $\eps >0$ and consider an open covering $\set{V_i}_{i=0}^\infty$ of $U$ with $V_0 =\emptyset$ and $V_{i} \Subset V_{i+1}$. By @thm-partition we may obtain a partition of unity $\rho _i$ subordinate to the ``rings'' $U_{i}:= V_{i+1}\setminus \overline{V_{i-1}}$ (the trickery with the indices is so that the $U_i$ actually  cover $U$). Where we relabel so that
	
$$
\begin{aligned}
\supp{\rho _i}\subset U_i .
\end{aligned}
$$

	We have that $\rho_i u \in C_c^\infty({V_{i+1}})$ with $V_{i+1} \Subset U$ so by the local approximation in @thm-local we can find $n_i$ such that
	
$$
\begin{aligned}
\norm{\phi_{n_i}*(\rho_i u)-\rho_i u}_{W^{k,p}(U)}=\norm{\phi_{n_i}*(\rho_i u)-\rho_i u}_{V_{i+1}}\leq \frac{\eps }{2^i} .
\end{aligned}
$$

	Now we obtain the global approximation by taking
	
$$
\begin{aligned}
\varphi:= \sum_{i=1}^\infty \phi_{n_i}* (\rho_i u) \in C_{\mathrm{loc}}^\infty(U) .
\end{aligned}
$$

	As then
	
$$
\begin{aligned}
\norm{\varphi-u}
		_{W^{k,p}(U)}\leq\sum_{i=1}^\infty \norm{\phi_{n_i}*(\rho_i u)-\rho_i u }_{W^{k,p}(U)}\leq \sum_{i=1}^\infty \frac{\eps }{2^i}=\eps   .
\end{aligned}
$$

:::

Note that @thm-meyers is not strictly stronger than @thm-local as it is not, in general, possible to extend functions in $C^\infty_{\mathrm{loc}}(U)$ to $C_c^\infty(\R^n)$. As a corollary of Meyers-Serrin's theorem, we obtain an equivalent definition of $W^{k,p}(U)$.

::: {#exr-eq-def}

Let $U \subset \R^d$ be an open set and $p<\infty$. Then $W^{k,p}(U)$ is equal to the completion of  $C^\infty_{\mathrm{loc}}(U)\cap W^{k,p}(U)$ with the $\norm{\cdot }_{W^{k,p}(U)}$ norm.

:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}

Use that $W^{k,p}(U)$ is complete and that the completion of a metric space is unique.

:::

Before  @thm-meyers was proved, both our original definition  @def-sobolev-def (distributions with derivatives in $L^p(U)$) and the one in @exr-eq-def (closure of smooth functions with Sobolev norm) were used as the definition of $W^{k,p}(U)$. But it was unclear which was the ``correct'' Sobolev space. This debate was settled by Meyers and Serrin who proved that, as we just showed, both are equal.

We now show an example of how these kinds of density results can be useful. The following generalizes the second point of @prp-properties

::: {#exr-change-of-variables name="Change of variables"}

Let $V,U$ be open in  $\R^d$ and $\Phi: V \simeq U$ be bijective with $\Phi \in C^k(V \to \R^d), \Phi^{-1} \in C^1(U\to \R^d)$. Then for any $u \in  W^{k,p}(U)$ it holds that $u \circ\Phi \in W^{k,p}(V)$ and the usual chain rule holds. For example

$$
\begin{aligned}
\partial _i (u\circ \Phi) = \sum_{j=1}^d (\partial _i \Phi_j)(\partial _j u_j)\circ \Phi_j  .
\end{aligned}
$$ {#eq-chain}

:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}

Give yourself an $\eps$ of room.
By induction, it suffices to consider the case $k=1$.We can approximate $u$ on each compact  $K \subset U$ by a sequence of functions $u_n \in C_c^\infty(\R^d)$. For $u_n$ the equality @eq-chain holds. Furthermore, by a change of variables, ([-@eq-chain]) is continuous in $u\in W^{1,p}(\Omega)$ so we may pass to the limit and obtain ([-@eq-chain]) for $u$ on $K$. Since $K$ was any, the equality also holds on the whole of $U$.

:::

In @exr-change-of-variables it is important that $\Phi$ is diffeomorphic so that composition with $\Phi$ is continuous.

::: {#exr-12}

Show that the conclusion of @exr-change-of-variables is false if we only assume that $\Phi \in C^k(\R^d)$ and do not impose invertibility.

:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}

Divide by zero.

:::

Now we provide a final global approximation result in the case where the domain is smooth (see Appendix @sec-boundary for a review on manifolds with boundary) and bounded.

::: {#thm-global name="Global, smooth on boundary approximation"}

Let  $\Omega$ be a **bounded** open domain with  **$C^1$ boundary**, then for all $p<\infty$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{\restr{C_c^\infty(\R^d)}{\Omega}}= W^{k,p}(\Omega).
\end{aligned}
$$

:::

::: {.proof}

The idea will be to locally translate points close to the boundary further into $\Omega$ so that we can convolve with an approximation of unity and then recover the global case with a partition of unity. We begin by ``straightening the boundary''. That is, since $\partial \Omega$ is $C^1$,  given $x_0 \in \partial  \Omega$ there exists an open set $V \subset \R^d$ and a function  $\gamma \in  C^1(\R^{d-1})$ such that, relabeling  and flipping the last coordinate axis if necessary

$$
\begin{aligned}
V \cap \Omega = \set{x \in V :x_d  >\gamma (x_1,\ldots,x_{d-1})}  .
\end{aligned}
$$

By considering a translation in the last coordinate $e_d=(0,\ldots,0,1)$ and its mollification by an approximation of unity $\phi_n$

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_n(x):= u\qt{x+ \frac{2}{n}e_d }; \quad \varphi_n:= u_n *\phi_n .
\end{aligned}
$$

For $n$ big enough we have that  $\varphi$ is well defined ans smooth on $W_0:=B_{\frac{1}{n}}(x_0)$. Since translation is continuous on $L^p(U)$ for  $p \in [1,\infty)$ and by the behavior of differentiation with convolution  (see @prp-properties), for $n$ large enough

$$
\begin{aligned}
\norm{u -\varphi_n}_{W^{k,p}(W_0)}\leq \norm{u -u_n}_{W^{k,p}(W_0)}+\norm{u_n -u_n*\phi_n}_{W^{k,p}(W_0)}\leq \eps.
\end{aligned}
$$

Now, since $\Omega$ is bounded $\partial \Omega$ is compact we may extract a finite covering $\set{W_i:=B_{\frac{1}{n_i}}(x_i)}_{i=0}^n$ of  $\partial \Omega$ and functions $\set{\varphi_i}_{i=1}^n$ smooth on $W_i$ such that

$$
\begin{aligned}
\norm{u -\varphi_n}_{W^{k,p}(W_i)}\leq \frac{\eps}{n+2}  .
\end{aligned}
$$

Now we take an open set $W_{n+1} \Subset \Omega$ such that $\set{W_i}_{i=0}^{n+1}$ cover $\Omega$. By the local approximation of @thm-local we know we can approximate  $u$ on  $W_{n+1}$ by some $\varphi_{n+1} \in C_c^\infty(\R^d)$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\norm{u-\varphi_{n+1}}_{W^{k,p}(W_{n+1})}\leq\frac{\eps}{n+2} .
\end{aligned}
$$

Finally, we take a smooth partition of unity $\set{\rho _i}_{i=0}^{n+1}$ subordinate to $\set{W_i}_{i=0}^{n+1}$ and a bump function $\eta \in C_c^\infty(\R^d)$ which is equal to $1$ on  $\Omega$ and is supported on $\bigcup_{i=0}^{n+1} W_i$ (see @exm-bump-example2) and set

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varphi:= \eta \sum_{i=0}^{n+1} \rho _i \varphi_i \in  C^\infty_c(\Omega) .
\end{aligned}
$$

This gives the desired approximation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\norm{u-\varphi}_{W^{k,p}(\Omega)} \leq \sum_{i=0}^{n+1} \norm{\rho _i(u-\varphi_i)}_{W^{k,p}(W_i)} \leq  \eps  .
\end{aligned}
$$

This concludes the proof.

:::

In contrast to @thm-meyers, @thm-global shows that Sobolev functions on smooth bounded domains can be approximated by functions that are also **smooth on the boundary** of the domain $\Omega$. This will prove fundamental in the next section. Both to extend them to the whole of $\R^d$ and to restrict them to $\partial \Omega$.

## Extensions and restrictions {#sec-extension-section}

Using the approximation of Sobolev functions by functions smooth on the boundary we can extend  functions in $W^{k,p}(\Omega)$ to the whole of  $\R^d$. However, the extension is not unique.

::: {#thm-extension name="Extension theorem"}

Let $\Omega\subset \R^d$ be a **bounded** open set with **$C^k$** ** boundary ** where $k \in \N_+$.  Then for all $p \in [1,\infty)$ . Then, given an open set $W$ with $\Omega \Subset W$ there exists a continuous operator

$$
\begin{aligned}
E: W^{k,p}(\Omega)\to W^{k,p}(\R^d); \quad E: C^{k}(\Omega)\to C^k(\R^d).
\end{aligned}
$$

such that

$$
\begin{aligned}
Eu = \begin{cases}
u \quad & \text{ on  }  \Omega \\
0 \quad & \text{ on }  W^c
\end{cases}.
\end{aligned}
$$

We call $E u$ an  **extension**  of $u$ to $\R^n$.

:::

::: {.proof}

We work first in the upper half-space (the canonical example of a manifold with boundary)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Hh^d =\set{x=(x_1,\ldots,x_d) \in \R^d : x_d \geq 0} .
\end{aligned}
$$

That is, we suppose that there is some open set $\Omega' \subset  \R^d$ such that

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Omega=\Omega' \cap \Hh^d_{>0}.
\end{aligned}
$$

Where

$$
\begin{aligned}
\quad \Hh^d_{>0} =\mathrm{int}(\Hh^d)=\set{x=(x_1,\ldots,x_d) \in \R^d : x_d >0} .
\end{aligned}
$$

By @thm-global we also give ourselves an epsilon of room by supposing that $u \in C^k(\overline{\Omega})$. We define the extension of $u$ to $\Omega'$ as

$$
\begin{aligned}
Eu(x) = \begin{cases}
		        u(x) \quad                                 & x_{d}                                 \geq 0      \\
		        \sum_{j=1}^{k+1} a_j  u_j(x - j e_d) \quad & x_d                                           < 0
	        \end{cases}.
\end{aligned}
$$

We will have $Eu \in C^k(\Omega')$ as long as we can get the derivatives to match up on the boundary $\{x_d=0\}\cap \overline{\Omega'}$. That is,  as long as $a_j$ verify

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{j=1}^{k+1}(-j)^l a_j =1 , \quad l=0,1,\ldots,k .
\end{aligned}
$$

The above is a system of $k+1$ equations with $k+1$-unknowns $a_j$. Its matrix is the [Vandermonde matrix](<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vandermonde_matrix>) (which is invertible). As a result, the system may be solved to extend $u$. By the form of $Eu$ we have the bound

$$
\begin{aligned}
\norm{Eu}_{W^{k,p}(\Omega')} \leq c\norm{u}_{W^{k,p}(\Omega)} .
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $c:= (k+1)^{k+1}\max \set{1,\norm{a}_\infty}$. Working now in the general case for $\Omega$, we may cover the compact $\overline{\Omega}$ by a finite amount of bounded open sets $\Omega_i \subset W$ such that

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Phi_i : \Omega_i \xrightarrow{\sim} \Omega_i'\quad \text{and}  \quad \Phi_i : \Omega_i \cap \Omega \xrightarrow{\sim} \Omega_i' \cap \Hh^d .
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $\Omega_i'$ are open in  $\R^{d}$ and $\Phi_i$ are $C^k$ diffeomorphisms. By the previous case, we can extend  $u'_i:=u \circ \Phi_i^{-1} \in C^k (\Omega_i' \cap \Hh^d)$ to functions $\widetilde{u'_i} \in C^k(\Omega_i')$ and then transform back to the original space to get

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tilde{u_i}:= \widetilde{u'_i} \circ \Phi_i \in C^k(\Omega_i).
\end{aligned}
$$

Where

$$
\begin{aligned}
\norm{\tilde{u_i}}_{W^{k,p}(\Omega_i)}\lesssim  \norm{u_i}_{W^{k,p}(\Omega)}.
\end{aligned}
$$ {#eq-bound}

The hidden constant depending only on  $c,\norm{\Phi_i}_{C^k(\Omega_i)}\norm{\Phi_i^{-1}}_{C^k(\Omega_i')}$. Next, using a partition of unity subordinate to $\set{\Omega_i}_{i=1}^n$ we glue the local extensions together to form an extension to all of $\Omega$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tilde{u}:= \sum_{i=1}^n \rho _i{u_i} \in C^k(\Omega).
\end{aligned}
$$

The desired extension can now be obtained by multiplying $\tilde{u}$ with a bump function $\eta$ that is supported in $W$ and equal to  $1$ on  $\Omega$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Eu := \eta \tilde{u} \in C^k(\R^d).
\end{aligned}
$$

By ([-@eq-bound]) we have that

$$
\begin{aligned}
\norm{E u}_{W^{k,p}(\R^d)} \lesssim  \norm{u}_{W^{k,p}(\Omega)}.
\end{aligned}
$$

As a result, $E$ is a (bounded) linear operator. So far we had considered $u \in  C^k(\overline{\Omega})$. Now, since by @thm-global the space  $C^k(\overline{\Omega})$ is dense in $W^{k,p}(\Omega)$ we may extend  $E$ to a linear operator on the whole of $W^{k,p}(\Omega)$. A verification shows that $E$ is also bounded as an operator from  $C^k(\Omega)$ to  $C^k(\R^d)$. This concludes the proof.

:::

::: {#exr-restriction name="Restriction"}

Under the conditions of @thm-extension it holds that

$$
\begin{aligned}
W^{k,p}(\Omega)= \restr{W^{k,p}(\R^d)}{\Omega}; \quad C^{k}(\Omega)= \restr{C^{k}(\R^d)}{\Omega}.
\end{aligned}
$$

That is, functions in $W^{k,p}(\Omega),C^{k}(\Omega)$ are equal to the restriction of functions in $W^{k,p}(\R^d),C^{k}(\R^d)$ respectively.

:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}

Given $u \in W^{k,p}(\Omega)$ we can extend it to $Eu \in W^{k,p}(\R^d)$ by the just proved extension @thm-extension.  By definition $u= \restr{Eu}{\Omega}$. The case $u \in C^k(\R^d)$ is identical.

:::

Using extensions also gives us a way to define the Sobolev spaces $H^s(\Omega)$ when the exponent $s$ is real valued.

::: {#def-9}

Given a **bounded** open set $\Omega$ with **boundary** of type $C^k$ with $k \in \N_+$, we define for all real $s \in [0,k]$

$$
\begin{aligned}
H^s(\Omega):=\restr{H^s(\R^d)}{\Omega} .
\end{aligned}
$$

:::

To further generalize this definition to domains where restriction is not possible one needs to use [complex interpolation](<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Interpolation_space>) (see for example [@taylor2013partial] pages 321-333).

## Trace theorem

As we already discussed, a PDE often incorporates boundary information such as $\restr{u}{\partial \Omega}=0$. This is well defined if $u$ is continuous, however, if  $u \in  W^{k,p}(U)$, and is thus only defined **almost everywhere**, then $\restr{u}{\partial  U}$ is a priori not well defined. The following theorem remedies this issue.

::: {#thm-trace name="Trace"}

Let $\Omega$ be a **bounded** open set of $\R^d$ with $C^1$  **boundary**. Then, there exists a continuous linear operator

$$
\begin{aligned}
T: W^{1,p}(U)\to L^p(\partial U) .
\end{aligned}
$$

Such that $Tu =\restr{u}{\partial \Omega}$ for all $u \in C(\overline{\Omega}) \cap W^{1,p}(\Omega)$.

:::

::: {.proof}

As in previous results, the trick is to suppose first $u$ is smooth, work locally, and then obtain a global result using a partition of unity and the density in @thm-global.  \\

Given $x_0 \in \partial  \Omega$ we take a open set $U \subset \R^d$ containing $x_0$. Flattening out the boundary by $\Phi : U \simeq U'$ where necessarily the boundary is preserved

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Phi: U \cap  \partial \Omega\iso U' \cap \partial  \Hh^d,
\end{aligned}
$$

and using the extension @thm-extension to extend $u':= u\circ \Phi$ to $\widetilde{u}'$ with compact support $K \subset \R^d$  we obtain by the [divergence theorem](<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Divergence_theorem#:~:text=space%5Bedit%5D-,We,-are%20going%20to%20prove>)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{U'\cap \partial \Hh^d}\abs{u'}^p  \leq\int_{\partial \Hh^d} \abs{\widetilde{u}'}^p=\int_{\Hh^d} \partial_{d} \abs{\widetilde{u}'}^p\leq\int_{\Hh^d} p\abs{\widetilde{u}'}^{p-1}\abs{\partial_{d} u}   \lesssim \norm{\widetilde{u}'}_{W^{1,p}(\Hh^d)}^p  .
\end{aligned}
$$

Where in the second inequality we used the chain rule and in the last Hölder's inequality.
Since $\Phi^{-1}$ is $C^k$ and by the continuity of the extension we obtain what we are looking for in

$$
\begin{aligned}
\norm{u}_{L^p(U \cap \partial \Hh^d)}\lesssim \norm{\widetilde{u}'}_{W^{1,p}(\Hh^d)}\lesssim \norm{u'}_{W^{1,p}(U'\cap \Hh^d)}\lesssim  \norm{u}_{W^{1,p}(U\cap \Omega)}
\end{aligned}
$$ {#eq-estimate-local}

We had supposed $u$ smooth, now taking a finite covering $\set{U_i}_{i=1}^n$ of $\partial  \Omega$ (this is possible by compactness of $\partial  \Omega$) and taking a subordinate partition of unity  $\rho _i$ we conclude from ([-@eq-estimate-local])  that

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{\partial \Omega} \abs{u}^p  =\sum_{i=1}^n \int_{ U_i \cap \partial \Omega} \abs{u}^p \lesssim \sum_{i=1}^n \norm{u}_{W^{1,p}(U_i \cap \Omega)}^p =\norm{u}_{W^{1,p}(U)}^p .
\end{aligned}
$$

That is,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\norm{T u}_{L^p( \partial \Omega)}\lesssim  \norm{u}_{W^{1,p}(U)} .
\end{aligned}
$$

Using @thm-global to extend $T$ continuously to $W^{k,p}(\Omega)= \overline{\restr{C_c^\infty(\R^d)}{\Omega}}$ concludes the proof.

:::

::: {#def-10}

We define the **trace** of $u \in W^{k,p}(\Omega)$ as $Tu$. We also use the notation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\restr{u}{\partial \Omega}:= Tu.
\end{aligned}
$$

:::

To get an estimate on the trace we paid $1$-degree of regularity. We can do better and only pay ${1}/{p}$ degrees of regularity. This uses the theory of [Sobolev–Slobodeckij spaces](<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trace_operator#For_p_=_1:~:text=%5Bedit%5D-,A%20more,-concrete%20representation%20of>) which we will not develop here. In the case $p=2$ we can use the fractional Sobolev spaces $H^s(\Omega)$ to get the improved result.

::: {#thm-1}

For all real $s> 1 /2$ the trace operator is a continuous operator

$$
\begin{aligned}
T: H^s(\Omega) \to H^{s -\frac{1}{2}}(\Omega).
\end{aligned}
$$

:::

::: {.proof}

Straightening out the boundary and using the extension operator as in the trace @thm-trace, it is sufficient to work in the case where $u$ is smooth and defined on  $\Hh^{d}$.  By Fourier inversion, if we write $\xi=(\xi',\xi _d)$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\wh{Tu}(\xi ')=\int_{\R}\wh{u}(\xi) \d \xi _d .
\end{aligned}
$$

So, by Cauchy Schwartz

$$
\begin{aligned}
\abs{\wh{Tu}(\xi ')}^2\leq \int_{\R}\abs{\wh{u}(\xi)}^2\br{\xi }^{2s} \d \xi _d \int_{\R}\br{\xi }^{-2s} \d \xi _d.
\end{aligned}
$$ {#eq-f1}

The change of variables $\xi _d \to \br{\xi '}\xi _d$ shows that

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{\R}\br{\xi }^{-2s} \d \xi _d=\br{\xi' }^{-2(s-\frac{1}{2})} \int_{\R}\br{\xi _d}^{-2s}\d \xi _d\lesssim  \br{\xi' }^{-2(s-\frac{1}{2})}
\end{aligned}
$$ {#eq-f2}

Where in the inequality it was used that $s> 1 /2$.     We deduce from ([-@eq-f1]) and ([-@eq-f2]) on taking norms that

$$
\begin{aligned}
\norm{u}_{H^{s-1 /2}(\R^{d-1})} \lesssim  \norm{u}_{H^s(\R^d)}.
\end{aligned}
$$

Which concludes the proof.

:::

A particularly useful space of functions related to the trace operator is the following

::: {#def-11}

We define the space of **functions with trace zero** as

$$
\begin{aligned}
W^{k,p}_0(U):=\overline{C_c^\infty(U)}\subset W^{k,p}(U).
\end{aligned}
$$

Where the closure is with respect to the topology on $W^{k,p}(U)$.

:::

::: {#exr-trace-0}

Show that for all $u \in W^{k,p}_0(\Omega)$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\restr{u}{\partial \Omega}=0.
\end{aligned}
$$

:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}

Use the continuity of the trace operator.

:::

The converse to @exr-trace-0 holds but is far from trivial.

::: {#prp-2}

Let $\Omega$ be a **bounded** open set with $C^1$  **boundary**. Then

$$
\begin{aligned}
W^{k,p}_0(\Omega)=\set{u \in W^{k,p}(U): Tu=0} .
\end{aligned}
$$

:::

The proof is very technical, see [@evans2022partial] page 274 for the details.\\

Being able to approximate functions in $W_0^{k,p}(U)$ by smooth functions compactly supported  **inside of** $U$  gives us many more tools. For example, a function $u$ in $W^{k,p}_0(U)$ can be extended by zero to obtain an element $\tl{u}$ in $W^{k,p}(\R^d)$ even for non-smooth unbounded domains.

::: {#exr-13 name="Extension trace 0"}

Let $U$ be an open set,     and define

$$
\begin{aligned}
\widetilde{u}:=\begin{cases}
u & \text{ on } U   \\
0 & \text{ on } U^c
\end{cases}.
\end{aligned}
$$

Then

$$
\begin{aligned}
E: W_0^{k,p}(U) \to W^{k,p}(\R^d); \quad  u \to \widetilde{u}
\end{aligned}
$$

is a linear with $\norm{E}=1$.

:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}

It is immediate that $\norm{\widetilde{u}}_{W^{k,p}(\R^d)}=\norm{\widetilde{u}}_{W^{k,p}(U)}$ for $u \in C_c^\infty(U)$. As a result, we can extend $E$ by density to the closure $C_c^\infty(U)$ in $W^{k,p}(U)$. Which by definition is  $W^{k,p}_0(U)$.

:::

::: {#rem-4}

The fact that we can extend functions in $W^{k,p}_0(U)$ for arbitrary  $U$ allows one to derive results that when stated for the whole of $W^{k,p}(U)$ require $U$ to be smooth so that it is possible to extend $U$.

:::

::: {#exr-14 name="Integration by parts 1"}

Let $U$ be any open set and consider $u \in  W^{1,p}_0(U), v \in W^{1,p'}(U)$ then

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{U} (\partial _iu) v = -\int_{U}u \partial _i v  .
\end{aligned}
$$

:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}

Give yourself an epsilon of room and take limits.

:::

::: {#exr-15 name="Integration by parts 2"}

Let $\Omega$ be a **bounded** open set of $\R^d$ with $C^1$  **boundary** and consider $u \in  W^{1,p}(\Omega), v \in W^{1,p'}(\Omega)$ then

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{\Omega} (\partial _iu) v = -\int_{\Omega}u \partial _i v+ \int_{\partial \Omega} u v **n**_i .
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $**n**$ is the outward pointing unit normal vector to $\partial \Omega$.

:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}

Give yourself an epsilon of room, apply the [divergence theorem](<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Divergence_theorem#:~:text=space%5Bedit%5D-,We,-are%20going%20to%20prove>) and take limits.

:::

## Sobolev embeddings and inequalities

Sobolev inequalities are relationships that bound the norm of $u$ in different function spaces depending on how differentiable and integrable $u$ is. For example, such a relationship could look like

$$
\begin{aligned}
\norm{u}_{W^{ l,p^* }(\Omega)}\leq \norm{u}_{W^{k,p}(\Omega)}.
\end{aligned}
$$ {#eq-example}

By considering the rescaling $u(\lambda x)$, performing a change of variables, and taking $\lambda$ to $\infty$ we see that for such a relationship to hold it is necessary that

$$
\begin{aligned}
l-     \frac{d}{p^*}=k-\frac{d}{p}  .
\end{aligned}
$$ {#eq-conjugate}

The case $k=l+1$ gives rise to the following definition

::: {#def-12}

The **Sobolev conjugate ** of $1\leq p<d$ is $p^*$ defined by

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{p^*}= \frac{1}{p} -\frac{1}{d} .
\end{aligned}
$$

:::

Note that $p<p^*$. The idea behind inequalities such as ([-@eq-example])  is to cash in some differentiability for some integrability. The main results used to do this are based on the fundamental theorem of calculus. First, we need the following lemma.

::: {#lem-whitney name="Loomis-Whitney inequality"}

Let $d \geq 1$, let $f_1, \ldots, f_d \in L^p\left(\mathbb{R}^{d-1}\right)$ for some $p \in (0,\infty]$, and define

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_d\left(x_1, \ldots, x_d\right):=\prod_{i=1}^d f_i\left(x_1, \ldots, x_{i-1}, x_{i+1}, \ldots, x_d\right).
\end{aligned}
$$

Then,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\norm{F_d}_{L^{p/(d-1)}}\leq\prod_{i=1}^d\left\|f_i\right\|_{L^p\left(\mathbb{R}^{d-1}\right)} .
\end{aligned}
$$ {#eq-loomis-ineq}

:::

::: {.proof}

The case $d=2$ is immediate by Fubini. The general case follows from induction on  $d$ . We write $(x_1,\ldots x_{d+1})=(x',x_{d+1})$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \norm{F_{d+1}}_{L^{p /d}(\R^{d+1})}  =\qt{\int_{\R}\qt{\int_{\R^d} F_d(x)^{\frac{p}{d}} f_{d+1}(x')^{\frac{p}{d}} \d x'  }  \d x_{d+1} }^{\frac{d}{p} }          \\
	 & \leq \qt{\int_{\R}         \qt{\int_{\R^d} F_d(x)^{\frac{p}{d-1}} \d x'  }^{\frac{d-1}{d}}\d x_{d+1} }^{\frac{d}{p} }\norm{f_{d+1}}_{L^p(\R^d)}      \notag      \\
	 & \leq \qt{\int_{\R} \prod_{i=1}^d\left\|f_i(x_{d+1})\right\|_{L^p\left(\mathbb{R}^{d}\right)}^{\frac{p}{d}} \d x_{d+1}}^{\frac{d}{p}}\norm{f_{d+1}}_{L^p(\R^{d})}
\end{aligned}
$$ {#eq-induc}

Where in the first inequality we applied Cauchy-Schwartz with $q= d /(d-1), q' =d$. Now applying the general version of Hölder's
$$
\begin{aligned}
\norm{g_1\cdots g_n }_{L^1} \leq \norm{g_1}_{L^{p_1}}\cdots\norm{g_1}_{L^{p_n}}; \quad  \frac{1}{p_1}+\cdots \frac{1}{p_n}=1\notag          .
\end{aligned}
$$

to $g_i:= \norm{f_i(\cdot )}^{\frac{p}{d}}_{L^p(\R^{d})} \in L^{d}(\R)$ gives

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{\R}\prod_{i=1}^{d}g_i \leq \prod_{i=1}^{d} \norm{g_i}_{L^d(\R)}    .
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting into ([-@eq-induc]) concludes the proof.

:::

Using the Loomis-Whitney inequality and the fundamental theorem of calculus gives us our first Sobolev inequality

::: {#thm-est1 name="Sobolev-Gagliardo-Niremberg"}

Given $1 \leq p<d$ it holds that

$$
\begin{aligned}
\norm{u}_{L^{p^*}(\R^d)}\lesssim  \norm{\nabla u}_{L^p (\R^d)}.
\end{aligned}
$$

:::

::: {.proof}

By density (see @thm-approx-rd), it is enough to take $u \in C_c^\infty(\R^d)$. Applying the fundamental theorem of calculus gives

$$
\begin{aligned}
\abs{u}^m=\int_{-\infty}^\cdot \partial _i \abs{u}^m \d x_i  \leq m\int_{\R}\abs{u}^{m-1}\abs{\partial _i u} \d x_i=: f_i , \quad\forall i=1,\ldots,d.
\end{aligned}
$$

Multiplying all these inequalities together gives

$$
\begin{aligned}
\abs{u}^{md} \leq \prod_{i=1}^{d} f_i   .
\end{aligned}
$$

Applying the Loomis-Whitney inequality ([-@eq-loomis-ineq])  ``with $p=1$'' and Hölder's inequality shows

$$
\begin{aligned}
\norm{u}_{L^{ md /(d-1)}(\R^d)}^{md}\lesssim  \prod_{i=1}^{d}  \norm{u^{m-1}\partial_iu}_{L^1(\R^d)} \leq \norm{u}_{L^{(m-1)p'}(\R^d)}^{(m-1)d}\norm{\nabla u}_{L^{p}(\R^d \to  \R^d)}^d
\end{aligned}
$$ {#eq-hard}

It remains to choose $m$ such that

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{md}{d-1}= (m-1)p' \implies m= \frac{(d-1)p^*}{d} =\frac{dp -p}{d-p} \geq 1.
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting into ([-@eq-hard]) gives

$$
\begin{aligned}
\norm{u}_{L^{ p^*}(\R^d)}^{m}\lesssim \norm{u}_{L^{p^*}(\R^d)}^{m-1}\norm{\nabla u}_{L^{p}(\R^d \to  \R^d)} .
\end{aligned}
$$

This concludes the proof.

:::

::: {#exr-est12}

Given $p<\frac{d}{k}$ define    $p^{k*}$ by $\frac{1}{p^{k*}}=\frac{1}{p}-\frac{k}{d}$. Then,        
$$
\begin{aligned}
\norm{u}_{L^{p^{k*}}(\R^d)} \lesssim  \norm{\nabla^k u}_{L^p(\R^d)}
\end{aligned}
$$

:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}

Apply induction on $k$ using @thm-est1. 

:::

The result can also be further [generalized](<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gagliardo%E2%80%93Nirenberg_interpolation_inequality>)
An estimate for the endpoint $d=p$ can also be achieved

::: {#thm-estt2}

It holds that

$$
\begin{aligned}
W^{1,d}(\R^d) \hookrightarrow L^q(\R^d) , \quad\forall  q \in [d,+\infty)
\end{aligned}
$$

:::

::: {.proof}

Setting $p=d$ in our estimate  ([-@eq-hard]) gives

$$
\begin{aligned}
\norm{u}_{L^{ m d/(d-1)}(\R^d)}^{m}\lesssim  \norm{u}_{L^{(m-1)d /(d-1)}(\R^d)}^{m-1}\norm{\nabla u}_{L^{d}(\R^d \to  \R^d)} .
\end{aligned}
$$

Applying Young's product inequality with $p = \frac{m}{m-1}, p'= m$ and using that raising to a power is convex gives

$$
\begin{aligned}
\norm{u}_{L^{ m d/(d-1)}(\R^d)}\lesssim \norm{u}_{L^{(m-1)d/(d-1)}(\R^d)}+\norm{\nabla u}_{L^{d}(\R^d \to  \R^d)} , \quad\forall  m \geq 1.
\end{aligned}
$$ {#eq-22}

Taking $m=d$ above gives

$$
\begin{aligned}
\norm{u}_{L^{d^2 /(d-1)}(\R^d)} \lesssim  \norm{u}_{W^{1,d}(\R^d)}.
\end{aligned}
$$

We also trivially have $\norm{u}_{L^d(\R^d)} \leq\norm{u}_{W^{1,d}(\R^d)}$ so by [interpolation](<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Riesz%E2%80%93Thorin_theorem#:~:text=%5Bedit%5D-,First,-we%20need%20the>) we can extend the inequality to

$$
\begin{aligned}
\norm{u}_{L^{q}(\R^d)} \lesssim  \norm{u}_{W^{1,d}(\R^d)} , \quad\forall q \in \qb{d,\frac{d^2}{d-1}}.
\end{aligned}
$$ {#eq-step-1}

We now iterate, taking $m=d+1$ gives

$$
\begin{aligned}
\norm{u}_{L^{ (d+1)d/(d-1)}(\R^d)}\lesssim \norm{u}_{L^{d^2/(d-1)}(\R^d)}+\norm{\nabla u}_{L^{d}(\R^d \to  \R^d)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Which combined with ([-@eq-step-1]) and interpolating gives

$$
\begin{aligned}
\norm{u}_{L^{q}(\R^d)} \lesssim  \norm{u}_{W^{1,d}(\R^d)} , \quad\forall q \in \qb{d,\frac{(d+1)d}{d-1}}.
\end{aligned}
$$

Iterating this process (taking $m=d+2,\ldots, m=d+k$ in ([-@eq-22])) shows that    
$$
\begin{aligned}
\norm{u}_{L^{q}(\R^d)} \lesssim  \norm{u}_{W^{1,d}(\R^d)} , \quad\forall q \in \qb{d+k,\frac{(d+k)d}{d-1}}.
\end{aligned}
$$

From this, we conclude the result.     

:::

::: {#exr-est22}

It holds that

$$
\begin{aligned}
W^{k,\frac{d}{k}}(\R^d) \hookrightarrow L^q(\R^d) , \quad\forall  q \in [d /k,+\infty)
\end{aligned}
$$

:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}

Use induction on $k$  with @thm-estt2. 

:::

Note that the constant in our above estimate blows up on iterating. As a result, we do not expect

$$
\begin{aligned}
W^{1,d}(\R^d) \hookrightarrow L^\infty(\R^d).
\end{aligned}
$$

This holds if and only if $d=1$. Otherwise, we require more integrability. However, this extra integrability can be converted into regularity in the style of [Sobolev spaces](<../../PDEs/1. The Fourier transform, tempered distributions and Sobolev spaces/index.qmd>). First, we recall the following definition

::: {#def-13}

Let $U \subset \R^d$ be open and $\gamma \in \R_+$, we define the **Holder space**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C^{k,\gamma }(\R^d):= \set{u \in C^k(U): \norm{u}_{C^{k,\gamma }(U)}<\infty} .
\end{aligned}
$$

Where the Holder norm is defined as

$$
\begin{aligned}
\norm{u}_{C^{0,\gamma }(U)} & := \sup_{x \neq y \in \R^d} \frac{\abs{u(x)-u(y)} }{\abs{x-y}^\gamma  }                \\
\norm{u}_{C^{k,\gamma }(U)} & :=\norm{u}_{C^{k}(U)}+\sum_{\abs{\alpha}= k } \norm{D^\alpha u}_{C^{0,\gamma }(U)}   .
\end{aligned}
$$

:::

For $\gamma =1$ the $C^{k,\gamma }$ is the space of functions with bounded derivatives up to order $k$ and whose $k$-th order derivatives are Lipschitz continuous.

::: {#thm-est3 name="Morrey"}

Let $p>d$ and set $\gamma=1-\frac{d}{p}$. Then, the following inclusion is continuous

$$
\begin{aligned}
W^{1,p}(\R^d) \hookrightarrow C^{0,\gamma }(\R^d).
\end{aligned}
$$

:::

The proof is technical and can be found in [@brezis2011functional] page 282.

As is logical, as $p$ approaches  $d$ from above the extra differentiability we get goes to  $0$. Furthermore, no matter how much integrability we cash in, we can never get more differentiability than we started with, so $\gamma \to 1$ as $p \to \infty$.

::: {#exr-est32 name="Sobolev regularity"}

Let $p>d$ and set $\gamma=1-\frac{d}{p}$. Then, the following inclusion is continuous

$$
\begin{aligned}
W^{k,p}(\R^d) \hookrightarrow C^{k,\gamma }(\R^d).
\end{aligned}
$$

:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}

This holds for the case $k=1$. We now proceed by induction. Since $\nabla u \in W^{k-1,p}(\R^d \to  \R^d)$ by hypothesis of induction we obtain

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla u \in C^{k-1,\widetilde{\gamma }}(\R^d \to \R^d); \quad \widetilde{\gamma }:= k-1 -\frac{d}{p} .
\end{aligned}
$$

In consequence,

$$
\begin{aligned}
u \in C^{k-1+1,\widetilde{\gamma}+1}(\R^d )=C^{k,\gamma}(\R^d ) .
\end{aligned}
$$

:::

Combining the three results gives

::: {#thm-rellich name="Rellich-Kondrachov"}

Let $\Omega$ be **bounded** with $C^k$ boundary, then the following  inclusions are continuous

$$
\begin{aligned}
& W^{k,p}(\Omega) \hookrightarrow L^q(\Omega) ,           \quad\forall q \in [1,p^{k*})                              & \text{ and }     p<\frac{d}{k} \\
& W^{k,p}(\Omega) \hookrightarrow L^q(\Omega) ,           \quad\forall q \in [p,\infty)                              & \text{ and }   p=\frac{d}{k}   \\
& W^{k,p}(\Omega) \hookrightarrow C^{k,\gamma }(\overline{\Omega}) \hookrightarrow C^{k}(\overline{\Omega}) ,  \quad & \text{ and }  p>\frac{d}{k}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $p^{k*}$ is defined by the relation $\frac{1}{p^{k*}}=\frac{1}{p}-\frac{k}{d}$ and $\gamma =1 -\frac{d}{p}$. Furthermore, the first, second, fourth, and third composed with fourth inclusions are [compact](<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Compact_embedding>).

:::

::: {#rem-5}

The continuous embeddings above also hold for $\Omega = \R^d$ (by the Sobolev inequalities in Exercises @exr-est12,@exr-est22,@exr-est32). However, the **compact** embeddings require $\Omega$ to be bounded. Indeed, on $\R^d$ one can construct bounded sequences in $W^{k,p}(\R^d)$ whose mass escapes to infinity, precluding any convergent subsequence.

:::

::: {.proof}

The fact that the above inclusions are continuous follows by our three Sobolev inequalities in Exercises @exr-est12,@exr-est22,@exr-est32 together with the extension @thm-extension. 

The compactness of the embeddings requires reduce to showing that the unit ball in each of the embedded spaces is [equicontinuous](<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Equicontinuity>).

1. For the first inclusion we will use [Fréchet–Kolmogorov's](<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fr%C3%A9chet%E2%80%93Kolmogorov_theorem>) theorem. First we note that we can suppose $q>p$ as  $L^q(\Omega) \hookrightarrow L^p(\Omega)$ (since $\Omega$ is bounded). Let $B_1$ be the unit ball in $W^{k,p}(\Omega)$. By continuity of the inclusion $B_1$ is bounded in $L^q(\Omega)$ and it only remains to show that  $B_1$ is equicontinuous. We have that

    $$
    \begin{aligned}
    \norm{\tau_h u-u}_{L^p(\Omega)}\leq \norm{\nabla u}_{L^p(\Omega)}\abs{h} , \quad\forall u \in W^{1,p}(\Omega) .
    \end{aligned}
    $$

    Where the above is known to be true for smooth functions by the fundamental theorem of calculus and extends by density to $W^{k,p}(\Omega)$ (we recall translation is continuous on $L^p$ for  $p<\infty$). The above also holds for $p^{k*}$ and since $p<q<p^{k*}$ we can write

    $$
    \begin{aligned}
    \frac{1}{q}=\frac{\alpha}{p}+\frac{1-\alpha}{p^{k*}}   .
    \end{aligned}
    $$

    By [interpolation](<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Riesz%E2%80%93Thorin_theorem#:~:text=the%20sumset%20formulation.-,Riesz%E2%80%93Thorin,-interpolation%20theorem%C2%A0%E2%80%94%C2%A0>) we obtain that

    $$
    \begin{aligned}
    \norm{\tau_h u-u}_{L^q(\Omega)}\leq \norm{\nabla u}_{L^p(\Omega)}^\alpha\norm{\nabla u}_{L^{p^{k*}}(\Omega)}^{1-\alpha}\abs{h} , \quad\forall u \in W^{1,p}(\Omega) .
    \end{aligned}
    $$

    This shows equicontinuity and concludes the first case.

2. The compactness of the second inclusion is proved identically.

3. For the compactness of the last inclusion we use [Arzelà–Ascoli theorem](<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arzel%C3%A0%E2%80%93Ascoli_theorem#:~:text=%2C%20%C2%A7IV.6.7>) on a ``derivative by derivative basis''. By definition of Hölder norm,

    $$
    \begin{aligned}
    \abs{D^\alpha u(x)- D^\alpha u(y)} & \lesssim  \norm{u}_{C^{k}(\R^d)}\abs{x-y} ,                 &  & \quad\forall \abs{\alpha}<k  \\
    \abs{D^\alpha u(x)- D^\alpha u(y)} & \lesssim  \norm{u}_{C^{k,\gamma }(\R^d)}\abs{x-y}^\gamma  , &  & \quad\forall \abs{\alpha}=k.
    \end{aligned}
    $$

    As a result, for each $\abs{\alpha}\leq k$ the family

    $$
    \begin{aligned}
    A_\alpha:=\set{D^\alpha u: u \in B_1 \subset C^{k,\gamma }(\overline{\Omega})},
    \end{aligned}
    $$

    is equicontinuous. So by Arzelà–Ascoli we may extract a sequence $u_{n}$ such that $D^\alpha u_n$ converges uniformly to some $u^{(\alpha)}$. By the fundamental theorem of calculus we conclude that $u^{(\alpha)}=D^\alpha u^{(0)}$ and as a result $u_n \to u \in C^k(\overline{\Omega})$. That is, the unit ball $B_1 \subset C^{k,\gamma }$ is sequentially compact and thus compact when embedded in  $C^k(\overline{\Omega})$. This proves this point.

4. The composition of the inclusions on the last line is compact as the composition of a compact and a continuous operator is compact.    This concludes the proof.

:::

As a particular case of the above we obtain the following simpler looking corollary

::: {#cor-simple-embedding}

Let $q< p^{k*}$, then it holds that following inclusion is compact

$$
\begin{aligned}
W^{k,p}(\Omega) \hookrightarrow L^q(\Omega).
\end{aligned}
$$

In particular, for all $p \in [1, \infty]$, the following inclusion is compact

$$
\begin{aligned}
W^{k,p}(\Omega) \hookrightarrow L^p(\Omega).
\end{aligned}
$$

:::

::: {.proof}

For any of the cases in Rellich-Kondrachov's @thm-rellich, the condition on $q$ holds. The first part of the result then follows as  $C^k(\overline{\Omega}) \hookrightarrow L^q(\Omega)$ and the second part from the fact that  $p< p^{k^*}$.

:::

Compact embeddings in spaces of higher order can be obtained through the following.

::: {#prp-3 name="Higher degree embeddings"}

Let  $k,l \in \N_0$ and $p,q \in [1,\infty]$ be such that

$$
\begin{aligned}
W^{k,p}(\Omega)\hookrightarrow L^q(\Omega)
\end{aligned}
$$

is compact. Then, the inclusion

$$
\begin{aligned}
W^{k+l,p}(\Omega)\hookrightarrow W^{l,q}(\Omega)
\end{aligned}
$$

is also compact.

:::

::: {.proof}

Let $u_n \in W^{k+l,p}(U)$ be a bounded sequence. By assumption $W^{k,p}(\Omega)$ is compact in  $L^q(\Omega)$, so we may extract a subsequence $u_{n_k}$ such that, for  $\abs{\alpha} \leq l$,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{k \to \infty}D^\alpha u_{n_k} = u^{(\alpha)} \in L^q(\Omega) .
\end{aligned}
$$

Using the same technique as in @thm-completeness shows that $u^{(\alpha)}= D^{\alpha}u$. This shows that $u_{n_k} \to u$ in $W^{l,q}(\Omega)$ and, by the equivalence of compactness and sequential compactness, concludes the proof.

:::

This result can be combined directly with Rellich-Kondrachov @thm-rellich or any other embedding. For example, when combined with @cor-simple-embedding we get

::: {#cor-1}

Let $q< p^{k*}$, then it holds that following inclusion is compact

$$
\begin{aligned}
W^{k+l,p}(\Omega) \hookrightarrow W^{l,q}(\Omega).
\end{aligned}
$$

In particular, for all $p \in [1, \infty]$, the following inclusion is compact

$$
\begin{aligned}
W^{k+l,p}(\Omega) \hookrightarrow W^{l,p}(\Omega).
\end{aligned}
$$

:::

The main utility of all these compact embeddings is that given a sequence $u_n$ whose derivatives are bounded in certain  $L^p$ norms we can extract convergent subsequences in appropriate spaces. We end this post (modulo appendices) with one of the most useful inequalities which we will make use of in future posts.

::: {#thm-2 name="Poincaré inequality"}

Let $u \in W^{1,p}_0(U)$ where $U$ is bounded in one direction. Then

$$
\begin{aligned}
\norm{u}_{W^{1,p}(U)}\leq C\norm{\nabla u}_{W^{1,p}(U \to \R^d)}.
\end{aligned}
$$

:::

::: {.proof}

By density (which holds by definition of $W^{k,p}_0(U)$) of it is sufficient to reason for $u \in C_c^\infty(U)$ and pass to the limit. By relabeling we may suppose $U$ is bounded along the $x_d$ axis. That is, for some finite $a<b$

$$
\begin{aligned}
U \subset \R^{d-1} \times [a,b].
\end{aligned}
$$

The idea is to use the fundamental theorem of calculus together with the compact support of $u$. This allows us to rewrite

$$
\begin{aligned}
\abs{u(x)}= \abs{\int_{a}^{x_d} \partial_d u(x',t) \d t} \leq \qt{\int_{\R}\abs{\partial _d u(x',t)}^p \d t}^\frac{1}{p}(x_d-a)^{\frac{1}{p'}}  .
\end{aligned}
$$

Taking $L^p(\R^d)$ norms above gives

$$
\begin{aligned}
\norm{u}_{L^p(\R^d)}\leq \norm{\partial _du}_{L^p(\R^d)}\frac{p'}{p+p'}(b-a)^{\frac{p+p'}{p'}} \lesssim  \norm{\nabla u}_{L^p(\R^d \to \R^d)} .
\end{aligned}
$$

This concludes the proof.    

:::

\appendix

## Convolutions and regularization

The convolution of two functions $f,g$ can be thought of as ``blurring''  $f$ by averaging it against $g$. In the case where  $g$ is smooth, this blurring has the effect of smoothing out any sharp edges and irregularities in  $f$. This allows us to approximate irregular functions by smooth ones and serves as an important technical tool in our analysis.

::: {#def-14 name="Convolution of functions"}

Given $f$ and $g$ we define the **convolution** of $f,g$ to be
the function $f *g$

$$
\begin{aligned}
f *g := \int_{\R^d} f(y)g(x-y) \d y
\end{aligned}
$$ {#eq-convo}

:::

The definition given by ([-@eq-convo]) is purposefully vague. We still need to specify what spaces $f,g$ belong to so that  $f*g$ is a well-defined element (of a further unspecified space). This can be done as follows.

::: {#prp-young name="Young's convolution inequality"}

Consider the definition in ([-@eq-convo]) and let $p,q,r \in  [1, \infty]$. Then it holds that

1. If $f \in L^1(\R^d)$ and $g \in L^p(\R^d)$ then $f*g \in L^p(\R^d)$ with

    $$
    \begin{aligned}
    \norm{f*g}_{L^p(\R^d)}\leq \norm{f}_{L^1(\R^d)}\norm{g}_{L^p(\R^d)}.
    \end{aligned}
    $$

2. Suppose that

    $$
    \begin{aligned}
    \frac{1}{p}+\frac{1}{q}=1+\frac{1}{r}; \quad f \in L^p(\R^d); \quad g \in L^q(\R^d)    .
    \end{aligned}
    $$

    Then $f*g \in L^r(\R^d)$ with

    $$
    \begin{aligned}
    \norm{f*g}_{L^r(\R^d)}\leq \norm{f}_{L^p(\R^d)}\norm{g}_{L^q(\R^d)}.
    \end{aligned}
    $$

3. In any of the above cases $f*g=g*f$.

:::

::: {.proof}

The first point follows from the triangle inequality for the Bochner integral (in this context this is also called Minkowski's integral inequality) as

$$
\begin{aligned}
\norm{\int_{\R^d}f(y)g(\cdot -y) \d y}_{L^p(\R^d)}\leq\int_{\R^d}\norm{f(y)g(\cdot -y) \d y}_{L^p(\R^d)}= \norm{f}_{L^1(\R^d)} \norm{g}_{L^p(\R^d)}.
\end{aligned}
$$

To see the second point fix $f$ and define the linear operator $T_fg:= f*g$. Then, for $g \in  L^1(\R^d)$ and $g \in  L^{p'}(\R^d)$ respectively

$$
