\begin{aligned}
\norm{T_f g}_{L^p(\R^d)} \leq \norm{f}_{L^p(\R^d)} \norm{g}_{L^1(\R^d)}; \quad    \norm{T_f g}_{L^\infty(\R^d)} \leq \norm{f}_{L^p(\R^d)} \norm{g}_{L^{p'}(\R^d)};  .
\end{aligned}
$$

Where the first inequality is point one and the second follows from Cauchy Schwartz. Now applying [Riesz-Thorin's interpolation theorem](< https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Riesz%E2%80%93Thorin_theorem#:~:text=the%20sumset%20formulation.-,Riesz%E2%80%93Thorin,-interpolation%20theorem%C2%A0%E2%80%94%C2%A0>) concludes the proof.

:::

The definition of convolution can be extended to even more settings, for example, suppose that $g$ is the density of some finite (possibly signed) measure $\mu$ and  $f$ is bounded, then

$$
\begin{aligned}
f*\mu(x):= f*g(x)= \int_{\R^d}f(x-y) g(y)\d y = \int_{\R^d} f(x-y)\d \mu (y) .
\end{aligned}
$$

::: {#def-15 name="Convolution of function with measure"}

Let $\mu$ be a finite signed Borel measure on $\R^d$ and  $f \in L^p(\R^d)$ then we define the convolution

$$
\begin{aligned}
f*\mu (x) :=\int_{\R^d} f(x-y)\d \mu (y) \in L^p(\R^d).
\end{aligned}
$$

:::

Note that, once more by the triangle inequality, the convolution is well-defined with

$$
\begin{aligned}
\norm{f* \mu }_{L^p(\R^d)} \leq \norm{f}_{L^p(\R^d)} \norm{\mu }_{TV} .
\end{aligned}
$$

Now, if we consider $f,g$ to be the densities of some finite (signed) measures  $\mu,\nu$ then we obtain that for bounded $h$

$$
\begin{aligned}
(h, \mu *\nu) & := \int_{\R^d} h(x) f*g(x) \d x = \int_{\R^d}\int_{\R^d}  h(x+y) f(x)  g(y)\d x \d y \\
	              & = \int_{\R^d \times\R^d} f(x+y) \d (\mu \otimes \nu)(x,y)\notag .
\end{aligned}
$$ {#eq-push}

That is, the convolution of $\mu$ with $\nu$ is the [pushforward](<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pushforward_measure>)  of the product measure $\mu \otimes \nu$ with the sum $S(x,y)$.

::: {#def-16 name="Convolution of measures"}

Let $\mu ,\nu$ be two finite signed measures on $\Bb(\R^d)$. Then the convolution of  $\mu *\nu$ is the pushforward

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu *\nu := S\# (\mu \otimes\nu).
\end{aligned}
$$

:::

The language of random variables can give some good motivation for this

::: {#exm-3}

Let $X,Y$ be random variables with law  $\mu ,\nu$ then $X+Y$ has law  $\mu *\nu$. Furthermore, if $X, Y$ are independent and $\mu,\nu$ are absolutely continuous with densities $f,g$ then $X+Y$ is absolutely continuous with density $f*g$.

:::

::: {.proof}

The first part is by definition of pushforward. To show that  $\mu *\nu$ has density $f*g$  it suffices to read the reasoning in equation  ([-@eq-push]) backward.

:::

Through the random variable interpretation, we also see that if $\mu,\nu$ have all their mass in $A, B$ then their convolution must have all its mass in  $A+B$. That is,

::: {#lem-support}

Let $f,g,\mu,\nu$ be such that the convolution is well-defined. Then

$$
\begin{aligned}
\supp{f*g}\subset \overline{\supp{f}+\supp{g}}; \quad \supp{\mu*\nu }=\overline{\supp{\mu }+\supp{\nu}}.
\end{aligned}
$$

:::

::: {.proof}

This follows directly from the definition of convolution.

:::

One technical point is that to define the convolution of two objects it is required that they be defined globally. For example if $U \subsetneq \R^d$, we can't convolve $f \in L^p(\R^d)$ with $g \in L_1(U)$ as the integral

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{\R^d}f(y)g(x-y) \d y
\end{aligned}
$$

requires we evaluate $f$ on all of  $\R^d$. One workaround is, if $\phi\in L^1(\R^d)$ with $\supp{\phi}\subset  \overline{B(0,\eps ) }$ we can extend $f$ to be equal to  some $g \in L^p(\R)$ outside of $U$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tilde{f}(x)=\begin{cases}
		             f(x) \quad & x \in U     \\
		             g(x) \quad & x \not\in U
	             \end{cases}.
\end{aligned}
$$

Then, the convolution $\tilde{f}*g$ is well defined and equal to

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tilde{f}*\phi(x) = \int_{B(x,\eps )\cap U} f(y)\phi(x-y) \d y+\int_{B(x,\eps )\cap U^c} g(y)\phi(x-y) \d y .
\end{aligned}
$$

As we can see, the convolution in general depends on how we extend $f$ outside of $U$. However, it is independent of the extension for $x$ in

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_\eps :=\set{x \in  U : d(x,\partial U)< \eps }.
\end{aligned}
$$

With

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tilde{f}*\phi(x) = \int_{B(x,\eps )} f(y)\phi(x-y) \d y , \quad\forall x \in  U_\eps   .
\end{aligned}
$$

For this reason, we will employ the following notation.

::: {#def-convolution-support}

Given $f \in L^p(U)$ and $\phi\in L^1(\R^d)$ with $\supp{\phi}\subset  \overline{B(0,\eps ) }$ we define $f*\phi \in L^p(U_\eps )$ as

$$
\begin{aligned}
f*\phi(x):= \int_{B(x,\eps )}f(y)\phi(x-y) \d y .
\end{aligned}
$$

:::

Convolution of distributions with test functions can also be considered. A similar reason to previously leads us to the following definition

::: {#def-17}

Let $T \in \Dd'(\R^d)$ and $\varphi \in C_c^\infty(\R^d)$. Then we define the convolution $T*\varphi \in \Dd^*(\R^d)$ by

$$
\begin{aligned}
T*\varphi(\phi):=T(\tl{\varphi}*\phi) \quad \text{ where } \tl{\varphi}(x):=\varphi(-x) .
\end{aligned}
$$

:::

In the above we can also swap all occurrences of $C_c^\infty(\R^d)$ and $\Dd'(\R^d)$ by $\Ss(\R^d)$ and $\Ss'(\R^d)$ respectively. An interesting fact is that the convolution of a distribution with a function is itself a function.

::: {#prp-4}

Convolution of distribution and test function is smooth:

1. Let $\varphi \in \Dd(\R^d), w \in \Dd'(\R^d)$ then $\omega * \varphi \in  C^\infty _{\mathrm{loc}}(\R^d)$.

2. Let $\varphi \in \Ss(\R^d), w \in \Ss'(\R^d)$ then $\omega * \varphi \in  C^\infty _{\mathrm{loc}}(\R^d)$.

:::

The previous definitions all go through word by word in the case where we substitute the domain from Euclidean space  $\R^d$ to a [LCA group with Haar measure](<../../PDEs/1. The Fourier transform, tempered distributions and Sobolev spaces/index.qmd>) $\mu$. For example

$$
\begin{aligned}
f*g(x):= \int_{G}f(y)g(x-y) \d \mu (y).
\end{aligned}
$$

A typical case is when $G=\T^d$ with the Lebesgue measure or $G=\Z^d$ with the counting measure. These respectively give

$$
\begin{aligned}
f*g(x)=\int_{\T^d}f(y)g(x-y) \d y; \quad      f*g(k):= \sum_{j\in \Z^d} f(j)g(k-j)  .
\end{aligned}
$$

The same results are also obtained. In fact, save the commutation $f*g=g*f$, the above results hold even if $G$ is not Abelian. In this case, one considers the [left or right Haar measure](<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Haar_measure#:~:text=%5Bedit%5D-,There,-is%2C%20up>). See for example [@fremlin2006measure] 444R.

## Smoothing in $L^p$ {#sec-smooth-section}

In this section, we examine how convolution can be used to approximate functions by smoother ones. This is of great practical use as it allows us to

1. Consider an appropriate space of functions for our problem, smooth or otherwise.

2. Perform formal manipulations using the standard rules of calculus as if all functions in this space were smooth and compactly supported until we obtain a desired result.

3. Pass to the limit to recover the expression for the whole class of functions.

A crucial tool in this program is the following:

::: {#def-app-def}

We say that a family of functions $\{\phi_n\}_{n= 1}^\infty \subset L^1(\R^d)$ is an **approximation to unity** if

- Norm 1: $\norm{\phi_n}_{L^1(\R^d)}=1$.

- Decreasing support: $\supp{\phi_n} \subset B(0, 1 /n)$.

If $\phi_n \in C_c^\infty(\R^d)$ we say that $\phi_n$ are smooth.

:::

::: {#rem-6}

The above definition is frequently also given letting the index set range over $\eps \in \R_+$ and taking $\supp{\phi_\eps } \subset B(0,\eps )$. This is equivalent and simply leads to taking $\eps \to 0$ instead of $n \to \infty$. 

:::

The first question is whether a smooth approximation to the identity exists. In the following example, we answer this in the affirmative.

::: {#exm-4}

Let $\varphi \in C_c^\infty(\R^d)$ then

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi_n:= \frac{n^d }{\norm{\varphi}_{L^1(\R^d)}}\varphi(nx)
\end{aligned}
$$

is a smooth approximation of the identity. Additionally,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varphi(x):=  \exp({\frac{1}{\abs{x}^2-1}}) 1_{B(0,1)} \in C_c^\infty(\R^d)
\end{aligned}
$$ {#eq-bump-example}

:::

By @prp-young $(L^1(\R^d),*)$ is a [Banach algebra](<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Banach_algebra>). However, it is a non-unital one. That is there does not exist an element $e$ such that

$$
\begin{aligned}
f*e=f , \quad\forall f \in L^1(\R^d).
\end{aligned}
$$

However, we will soon see that in a limiting sense, an identity for the convolution exists. First, we need the following lemma.

::: {#lem-uryshon}

The space $C_c(\R^d)$ is dense in $L^p(\R^d)$ for all $p \in [1,\infty)$.

:::

::: {.proof}

Consider $f \in  L^p(\R^d)$. If $f =1_A$ for some measurable set $A$ with finite measure then, by the outer and inner regularity of the Lebesgue measure we may take $U, K$ open and compact respectively with  $U \subset A \subset K$ and

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda  (K)-\lambda  (A) <\eps .
\end{aligned}
$$

Where we wrote $\mu$ for the Lebesgue measure on $\R^d$.
By [Urysohn's lemma](<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Urysohn%27s_lemma#:~:text=for%20any%20two-,non%2Dempty,-closed%20disjoint%20subsets>) there exists a continuous function $\varphi \in C_c(U)$ such that $\varphi \leq 1$ and $\varphi$ is $1$ on  $K$. Then,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\norm{f- \varphi}_{L^p(\R^d)} \leq \eps .
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the space of simple functions is dense in $L^p(\R^d)$ this concludes the proof.

:::

The name ``approximation of unity'' in Definition @def-app-def is justified by the following proposition.

::: {#prp-app-pn}

Let $f \in L^p(\R^d)$ where $p \in [1,\infty)$ and consider $g \in C_c(\R^d)$ and an approximation to unity $\phi_n$. Then it holds that

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{n \to \infty}g*\phi_n=g \in  C_c(\R^d);\quad \lim_{n \to \infty}f*\phi_n=f \in  L^p(\R^d).
\end{aligned}
$$

:::

::: {.proof}

Consider $\eps >0$. Using that $\phi_n$ has mass $1$ and is supported on  $B(0,1/n)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
g*\phi_n(x)-g(x) & = \int_{B(0, \frac{1}{n})}(g(x-y)-g(x)) \phi_n(y) \d y
	.
\end{aligned}
$$

Now taking norms and $n$ large enough gives

$$
\begin{aligned}
\norm{g*\phi_n-g}_{L^\infty(\R^d)} \leq \int_{B(0, \frac{1}{n})} \norm{g(\cdot -y) -g}_{L^\infty(\R^d)} \phi_n(y) \d y\leq \eps   .
\end{aligned}
$$ {#eq-nm}

Where in the last inequality we used that $g$ is uniformly continuous and $\phi_n$ has mass $1$. Since $\eps >0$ was any, this shows the first part of the proposition.

We now prove the second part. By @lem-uryshon we can choose $g$ such that

$$
\begin{aligned}
\norm{g-f}_{L^p(\R^d)}<\eps .
\end{aligned}
$$

Now, since $K_n:=\supp{g* \phi_n} \subset K + B(0, 1 /n)$, whose measure is bounded by some $M>0$, the inequality in  ([-@eq-nm]) shows that

$$
\begin{aligned}
\norm{g*\phi_n-g}_{L^p(\R^d)}^p \leq \int_{K_n} \eps^p   \d y\leq M\eps^p  .
\end{aligned}
$$

Now using the triangle inequality and  Young's convolution inequality @prp-young gives

$$
\begin{aligned}
\norm{f*\phi_n-f}_{L^p(\R^d)} & \leq\norm{(f-g)*\phi_n}_{L^p(\R^d)}+\norm{g*\phi_n-g}_{L^p(\R^d)}          \\
	                              & +\norm{g-f}_{L^p(\R^d)}\leq \eps + M^{\frac{1}{p}}\eps +\eps .
\end{aligned}
$$

This shows the second part and concludes the proof.

:::

The question is why would we want to approximate a function by its convolutions with some smooth functions the answer is given in the following two results.

::: {#prp-smooth name="Smoothing effect"}

Let $f \in L^1_{\text{loc}}(\R^d)$ and $\phi \in C_c^\infty(\R^d)$. Then $f*\phi \in C^\infty(\R^d)$ with

$$
\begin{aligned}
D^\alpha(f *\phi)=f*D^\alpha \phi , \quad\forall \alpha \in \N^d.
\end{aligned}
$$

Furthermore, if $f$ is compactly supported then  $f*\phi \in  C_c^\infty(\R^d)$.

:::

::: {.proof}

By induction, it suffices to consider the case $D^\alpha = \partial _i$ for some $1 \leq i \leq d$. This case can be proved by a [differentiation under the integral sign](<../../PDEs/1. The Fourier transform, tempered distributions and Sobolev spaces/index.qmd>) as, given $\abs{x}\leq M$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\partial _{x_i} f(y)\phi(x-y) \leq f(y)\norm{\phi}_{C^\infty(K)} 1_{K+B(0,M)}(y) \in L^1(\R^d)
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $K$ is the support of  $\varphi$.

:::

::: {#rem-local-smoothing name="Local smoothing"}

The smoothing effect also holds when $f$ is only defined on some open set $U$. Then, with the notation of Definition @def-convolution-support, $f*\phi \in C^\infty(U_\eps )$ with an identical proof showing

$$
\begin{aligned}
D^\alpha(f *\phi)=f*D^\alpha \phi \text{ on } U_\eps .
\end{aligned}
$$

:::

::: {#thm-density-thm}

It holds that

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{C_c^\infty(\R^d)}= L^p(\R^d); \quad \overline{C_c^\infty(\R^d)} = C_c(\R^d).
\end{aligned}
$$

Where the closures are respectively in the $L^p(\R^d)$ and the uniform topology $($given by $\norm{\cdot }_{\infty})$.

:::

::: {.proof}

This follows immediately from @prp-app-pn and @prp-smooth. 

:::

::: {#exr-16}

Let $U$ be an open subset of  $\R^d$, show that

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{C_c^\infty(U)}= L^p(U); \quad \overline{C_c^\infty(U)} = C_c(U).
\end{aligned}
$$

:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}

Let $f$ be the non-smooth function to approximate, multiply it first by a mollifier $\eta_n \in C_c^\infty(U)$ equal to $1$ in $V_n\subset U$ (see @exm-bump-example2). Convolve to get

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varphi_n:=(f \eta_n )*\rho _n.
\end{aligned}
$$

Show as in @thm-density-thm that $\varphi_n$ converges appropriately.

:::

The above can be generalized to non-Euclidean spaces

::: {#thm-3}

Let $(X,\mu )$ be a measure space such that $X$ is locally convex and Hausdorff and  $\mu$ is inner and outer regular. Then

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{C_c(X)} =L^p(X).
\end{aligned}
$$

Suppose additionally that $X$ is a group, that $\mu$ is the left or right Haar measure, and that there exists an approximation to unity $\phi_n$ on $X$. Then

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{C_c^\infty(X)} =L^p(X);\quad \overline{C_c^\infty(X)} =C_c(X).
\end{aligned}
$$

:::

::: {.proof}

The first part can be proved by the same method as in @lem-uryshon (note that Uryshon's lemma [still holds](<https://planetmath.org/ApplicationsOfUrysohnsLemmaToLocallyCompactHausdorffSpaces>) for LCH spaces). The second part follows by copying the proof of Propositions @prp-app-pn and @prp-smooth. 

:::

The assumption of the existence of an approximation of unity is perhaps the most delicate, but it can be applied for example in the following case.

::: {#cor-stone}

It holds that

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{C^\infty(\T^d)}= L^p(\T^d); \quad \overline{C^\infty(\T^d)} = C(\T^d).
\end{aligned}
$$

:::

Note that the second part of @cor-stone also follows from the [Stone-Weierstrass theorem](<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stone%E2%80%93Weierstrass_theorem>).
Similar results hold in the space of distributions.    

::: {#prp-5}

Let $T\in \Dd'(\R^d)$ and $\varphi_n$ be an approximation to unity. Then

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{n \to \infty} T* \phi_n= T \in \Dd'(\R^d).
\end{aligned}
$$

As a result, $\overline{C_c^\infty(\R^d)}=\Dd'(\R^d)$.

:::

The proof can be found in [@leoni2017first] page 308.

## Global to local and back again {#sec-local-to-global}

Often it is advantageous to work locally and then reason in the general case by some kind of approximation. A useful tool in this respect are **bump functions**.

::: {#def-18}

A **bump function** (also called **cutoff function**) is a function $\eta \in C_c^\infty(\R^n)$.

:::

Constructing bump functions that have some desired support is a tool we use frequently throughout. Here we provide two examples to show how this may be done. Other constructions are of course possible.

::: {#exm-bump-example2}

Given two open sets $V,U$ with  $V \Subset U$ there exists $\eta \in C_c^\infty(\R^d)$ with support in $U$, equal to  $1$ on  $V$ and with $0\leq \eta \leq 1$.

:::

::: {.proof}

Since $V \Subset U$, there exists a compact  $K$ with

$$
\begin{aligned}
V \subset K \subset U;\quad d_u:=d(U,K)>0; \quad d_V:=d(V,K)>0.
\end{aligned}
$$

Now, by [Urysohn's lemma](<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Urysohn%27s_lemma#:~:text=for%20any%20two-,non%2Dempty,-closed%20disjoint%20subsets>) there exists a continuous function  $f \in C_c(\R^n)$ such that $0\leq\varphi \leq 1$ and $\varphi$ is $1$ on  $K$.  If we now take an approximation of unity $\set{\phi_n}_{n=1}^\infty$  and choose $N$ large enough so that $\min \set{d_U,d_V} > \frac{1}{N}$ we can obtain the desired function as $\eta=f*\phi_{N}$.

:::

::: {#exm-5}

There exists a sequence of $\eta_n \in C_c^\infty(\R^d)$ such  that

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_n(x) =\begin{cases}
1 \text{ if } & \abs{x}\leq n  \\
0 \text{ if } & \abs{x}\geq 2n \\
\end{cases}.
\end{aligned}
$$

And $\norm{\eta_n}_{C^k(\R^d)} \leq \norm{\eta_1}$.

:::

::: {.proof}

Let $\eta_1 \in C_c^\infty(\R^d)$ be any (for example that of ([-@eq-bump-example])). Then we can take $\eta_n(x):=\eta(x /n)$.

:::

In the opposite direction. One is often in the situation where it is possible to derive some local properties for a given object (think manifolds). To recover a global result one needs some way to piece together the local results. A useful tool in this respect is **partitions of unity**.

::: {#def-19}

Given a manifold $M$ and an o pen covering $\{U_\alpha\}_{\alpha \in  J}$ of $M$ we say that  $\{\rho_i\}_{i\in  I}$ is a **partition of unity on $\{U_\alpha\}_{\alpha \in  J}$** if:

1. $\supp{\rho_i}\subset U_\alpha$ for some $\alpha \in J$.

2. For each $x \in M$, it holds that $x \in \supp{\rho_\alpha}$ for only a finite amount of $\alpha \in I$.

3. $\sum_{i \in I}\rho _i =1.$

:::

Partitions of unity are often used in differential geometry as follows

1. Work in some open subset $\R^n$ (or the upper half space $\R^n_+$ if our manifold has boundary) to prove the existence of some object $g$ with desired properties.

2. Cover the manifold $M$ with coordinate charts $U_\alpha$ and translate the euclidean result via the identification with $U_\alpha$ to obtain locally defined $g_\alpha$.

3. Obtain a globally defined object $g$ by using the partition of unity to piece together the local objects

    $$
    \begin{aligned}
    g=\sum_{\alpha \in I}\rho_\alpha g_\alpha .
    \end{aligned}
    $$

In addition to the approximation and extension theorems in Section @sec-extension-section, partitions of unity can be used to show that: every manifold has a [Riemannian metric](<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Riemannian_manifold>), show that a function is smooth on some none-open set $S \subset M$ iff it is the restriction of a smooth function defined on a neighborhood of $S$, prove the existence of an outward pointing vector on manifolds with boundary, define integration over an orientable manifold $M$, prove [Stoke's theorem](<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Generalized_Stokes_theorem>).

::: {#thm-partition}

Let $M$ be a smooth manifold (in particular we assume $M$ is Hausdorff), then every open covering $\set{U_\alpha}_{\alpha \in  J}$ has a partition of unity $\set{\rho _n}_{n=1}^\infty$.

:::

The proof is based on the existence of bump functions (something we have already proved for $\R^d$) and is straightforward in the case that $M$ is compact. The general case can be reduced to the compact setting by obtaining a covering by relatively compact open sets $\set{U_i}_{i=0}^\infty$ of $M$ such that 
$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{i} \Subset V_{i+1}.
\end{aligned}
$$

And then working with the compact $\overline{V_{i+1}} \setminus V_{i}$.    See [@tu2011manifolds] Appendix C for the details.

## Manifolds with boundary {#sec-boundary}

We will be defining differential equations on open domains $\Omega \subset \R^n$. In this case $\overline{\Omega}$ will be a ``manifold with boundary'' whose regularity will determine what results we have access to. The prototypical example of a manifold with a boundary is the upper half space

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Hh^d:=\set{x=(x_1,\ldots,x_d)\in \R^d: x_d \geq 0} .
\end{aligned}
$$

Here the inequality is not strict so that the boundary of $\Hh^d$ is included in itself. Since $\Hh^d$ is not open we need to define what is meant by saying that a function is differentiable on such a set.

::: {#def-smooth-non-open}

Let $S \subset \R^d$ be an arbitrary set. We say that a function $f:S \to \R$ is  **$k$-times differentiable at $p$** if there exists a function $\tl{f}:\R^d \to \R$ which is $k$ times differentiable at  $p$ and such that $\tl{f}=f$ on $S$.

:::

::: {#def-20}

If $f$ is  $k$-times differentiable at every point of  $S$ we say that $f \in C^k(S)$.

:::

::: {#exr-ext}

Show that $f\in  C^k(S)$ if and only if there exists an extension $\tl{f}\in C^k(\R^d)$ of $f$ which is equal to $f$ on $S$.

:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}

Use a partition of unity.

:::

A manifold with boundary $M$ is just a generalization of $\Hh^d$, where we impose that $M$ is ``locally equal'' to $\Hh^d$. That is, there exists a covering of $M$ by open sets  $V_\alpha$ and a collection of homeomorphisms with the **subspace topology** from $\Hh^d$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Phi_\alpha : V_\alpha \iso  \Phi(V_\alpha).
\end{aligned}
$$ {#eq-chart}

And where for compatibility we impose that for each $\alpha,\beta$ the function

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Phi_\beta \circ\Phi_\alpha^{-1}: \Phi_\alpha(V_\alpha\cap V_\beta ) \longmapsto \Phi_\beta (V_\alpha\cap V_\beta ).
\end{aligned}
$$ {#eq-diff}

Are diffeomorphisms on $\Hh^d$ (again with the subspace topology). Here $\Aa=\set{(U_\alpha,\Phi_\alpha)}$ is an **atlas** of $M$. We say that $\Aa$ is $C^k$ if the diffeomorphisms in ([-@eq-diff]) are $C^k$ (see Definition @def-smooth-non-open).

::: {#def-21}

We say that $(M,\Aa)$ is a **$C^k$ manifold with boundary** if $M$ is a second countable Hausdorff space and $\Aa$ is a $C^k$ atlas.

:::

The boundary of $M$ is its points that are mapped to the boundary $\set{x_d=0}$ in $\Hh^d$.

::: {#def-22}

Given a point $p \in M$ we say that $p$ is a **boundary point** if for some (and thus every chart) $\Phi_\alpha(p) \in \partial \Hh ^{d}$. We call the set of all boundary points the **boundary** of $M$ and denote it by $\partial M$.

:::

::: {#exr-17}

Show that the restricted atlas $\restr{\Phi_\alpha}{\partial M}$ makes $\partial M$ a $d-1$ dimensional manifold **without** boundary.

:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint"}

By definition of boundary

$$
\begin{aligned}
\restr{\Phi_\alpha}{\partial M}: V_\alpha \cap M \to \partial \Hh^{d-1} \simeq \R^{d-1}.
\end{aligned}
$$ {#eq-chart2}

And the coordinate changes are $C^k$ as the restriction of a $C^k$ map is $C^k$.

:::

In our case we will always take $M$ to be a subset of  $\R^d$, in this case, different variations of the above definition are possible. For example, by the inverse function theorem and @exr-ext, we can extend the functions $\Phi_\alpha$ to diffeomorphisms on $U_\alpha$ **open in $\R^d$** so that ([-@eq-chart])-([-@eq-chart2]) now read

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Phi_\alpha : U_\alpha \iso  \Phi(U_\alpha); \quad    \Phi_\alpha : U_\alpha \cap M \iso  \Phi_\alpha(U_\alpha) \cap \Hh^d .
\end{aligned}
$$ {#eq-alt0}

Additionally, by the implicit function theorem there exists for each coordinate set $U_\alpha$  functions $\gamma_\alpha \in C^k(\R^{d-1})$ such that, relabeling the coordinates and decreasing the size of $U_\alpha$ if necessary,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\partial M \cap U_\alpha & =\set{x \in U_\alpha : x_d=\gamma_\alpha(x_1,\ldots,x_{d-1}) }
\end{aligned}
$$ {#eq-alt1}

Let us write $x=(x',x_d)$, by the Taylor expansion

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Phi_\alpha(x',\gamma _\alpha(x')+\eps)=\Phi_\alpha(x)+\frac{\partial \Phi}{\partial x_d}(x)\eps +O(\norm{\eps }^2)  .
\end{aligned}
$$

We deduce that,  once more reducing the size of $U_\alpha$ if necessary and depending on the sign of $\partial _d \Phi$ on $U_\alpha$, one and only one of the following two hold

$$
\begin{aligned}
(M \setminus \partial M) \cap U_\alpha & =\set{x \in U_\alpha : x_d>\gamma_\alpha(x_1,\ldots,x_{d-1}) }        \\
	(M \setminus \partial M) \cap U_\alpha & =\set{x \in U_\alpha : x_d<\gamma_\alpha(x_1,\ldots,x_{d-1}) }\notag.
\end{aligned}
$$ {#eq-alt3}

The equivalent formulations in ([-@eq-alt0]) and in ([-@eq-alt1])-([-@eq-alt3]) are used in the main exposition. Finally, in our case, we will typically take $M =\overline{\Omega}$ where $\Omega \subset \R^d$ is some open set. In this case, we adopt the following terminology.

::: {#def-23}

We say that an open set $\Omega\subset \R^d$ has **$C^k$ boundary** of  $\overline{\Omega}$ is a $C^k$ manifold with boundary.

:::

In the above case, the topological and manifold boundaries of $\Omega$ necessarily coincide as homeomorphisms map topological boundaries to topological boundaries.

## References

::: {#refs}
:::
